---
title:
  "Shrinkage Estimation in Finance and Insurance: Portfolio Construction and
  Claim Severity Modelling"
subtitle: "SMM069 (2025-26) Group Coursework - Group 12"
author:
  - "Gaurav Agrawal"
  - "Benjamin Evans"
  - "Samyak Jain"
  - "Basmah Khan"
date: "`r format(Sys.Date(), '%d %B %Y')`"
output:
  bookdown::html_document2:
    self_contained: true
    css: style.css
    toc: true
    toc_float: true
    toc_depth: 3
    number_sections: true
    code_folding: hide
    theme: paper
    highlight: tango
    df_print: paged
    # runtime: shiny
    pandoc_args:
      - "--lua-filter=style_code.lua"
  bookdown::pdf_document2:
    includes:
      in_header: preamble.tex
    pandoc_args:
      - "--lua-filter=answer.lua"
      - "--lua-filter=style_code.lua"
    number_sections: true
    toc: true
    latex_engine: xelatex
    citation_package: natbib
    keep_tex: true
# fontsize: 10pt
bibliography: references.bib
---

```{=latex}
\newpage
```

```{=html}
<h1>Information</h1>

This R Markdown document was created as part of a group assignment for SMM069 at Bayes Business School, City St George's, University of London in Term 3 2025-26.

```

```{r asthetic-header, include=FALSE, echo=FALSE}
# ============================================================================ #
# Key Information ====
# SMM069 Advanced Predictive Analytics
# Group Coursework 2025-26
# Group:        Group 12
# Authors (in alphabetical order):
#   - "Gaurav Agrawal"
#   - "Benjamin Evans"
#   - "Samyak Jain"
#   - "Basmah Khan"
# Professor:    Prof Vali Asimit & Ms Ziwei Chen
# Institution:  Bayes Business School - City St George's, University of London
# Date:         09 Jul 2026
#
# Description:  Term 3 group project for SMM069 Advanced Predictive Analtyics
#               (100% of coursework grade - 100% of module grade).
#
# Dependencies:
#   - TBI
#
#   - tidyverse: loads dplyr, tidyr, ggplot2, purrr (& others)
#   - scales: to express portfolio weights as percentage
#   - patchwork: for combining multiple plots into one figure
#
# Acknowledgements
#   - TBI
#
# Note to Vali and Ziwei:
#   We originally planned to submit just an R file exported from this RMD,
#   hence this 'asthetic header'. We left it in as a legacy artefact after
#   deciding to submit our code as this single RMD file.
# ============================================================================ #
```

```{r setup-knitr, include=FALSE, echo=FALSE}
# Setup & configuration ====
#----------------------- Initial setup (knitr settings) -----------------------#
dir.create("fig", showWarnings = FALSE)

# Defaults common to all outputs
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo     = FALSE,
  message  = FALSE,
  warning  = FALSE,
  fig.align = "center",
  out.width = "100%",
  fig.path  = "fig/",
  dpi       = 300
)

# Output-specific settings
if (knitr::is_latex_output()) {
  knitr::opts_chunk$set(
    fig.width = 6,
    fig.height = 4,
    dev = "pdf",
    fig.pos = "!ht",
    out.extra = ""
  )
} else {
  knitr::opts_chunk$set(
    fig.width = 6,
    fig.height = 4,
    dev = "svglite"  # or "png"
  )
}
```

```{r setup-qol, include=FALSE, echo=FALSE}
#----------------------------- Clean environment ------------------------------#
rm(list = ls()) # Remove all objects
graphics.off() # Close all graphical devices
cat("\014") # Clean console
```

```{r load-dependencies, include=FALSE, echo=FALSE}
#------------------- Load dependencies / external libraries -------------------#
# --- Data Manipulation & Plotting ---
library(tidyverse) # loads dplyr, tidyr, ggplot2, purrr (& others)
library(patchwork) # for combining multiple plots into one figure

# --- Reporting & Tables ---
library(kableExtra) # for general tables
library(DT)         # for HTML interactive data tables
library(plotly)     # for interactive html plots
library(pander)     # for writing to pandoc

# --- Statistical Modelling & Mathematics ---
library(MASS)      # Support functions and datasets for applied statistics
library(glm2)      # Fitting generalised linear models
library(glmnet)    # Lasso and elastic-net
library(splines)   # Regression spline functions

# --- Course-Specific / Custom Packages ---
library(savvySh)   # Custom coursework package
library(savvyPR)   # Custom coursework package
library(savvyGLM)  # Custom coursework package

# tmp ====
library(readxl) # reading xlsx GICS sectors
library(PerformanceAnalytics)
library(xts)
library(reshape2)

# Estimator functions ====
# St_MSh_, D_MSh_, O_LSh_, T_LSh_, Wang_, BOP_, Jorion_estimator
source("All_eight_shrinkage_estimators.R")
source("cov1Para.R")

# Global env options ====
## Setting random seed ----
group_num <- 12 # seed 12 for group 12 ( ˶ˆᗜˆ˵ )
set.seed(group_num)
## disable scientific notation ----
options(scipen = 999) 
options(knitr.kable.NA = "---") # all NA -> dashes 
```

```{r custom-functions, include=FALSE, echo=FALSE}
#---------------------------- Custom QOL functions ----------------------------#
# BE Note: most of these are legacy functions from previous projects - just
# keep them here to have all in one contained file.
#####################################
# function: format p-values for text
# Usage (in-line): `r format_p_vals(ad_test_result$p.value)`
# Usage (console): format_p_vals(ad_test_result$p.value)
#####################################
format_p_vals <- function(p) {
  if (length(p) != 1L || is.na(p)) {
    stop("Error! p must be a single non-missing value")
  }
  if (p > 1) {
    stop("Error! Value greater than 1")
  }
  if (p < 0) {
    stop("Error! Value less than 0")
  }

  if (p >= 0.01) {
    paste0("= ", formatC(p, format = "f", digits = 2))
  } else if (p >= 0.001) {
    paste0("= ", formatC(p, format = "f", digits = 3))
  } else {
    "< 0.001"
  }
}
#####################################
# function: format confidence intervals for tables & text
# Usage (in-line): `r format_interval(el2_ci_normal_95[1], el2_ci_normal_95[2])`
# Usage (console): format_interval(el2_ci_normal_95[1], el2_ci_normal_95[2])
#####################################
format_interval <- function(lower, upper, digits = 3, small_interval = 5L) {
  paste0(
    "[",
    formatC(
      lower,
      format = "f",
      digits = digits,
      small.interval = small_interval,
      small.mark = " "
    ),
    ", ",
    formatC(
      upper,
      format = "f",
      digits = digits,
      small.interval = small_interval,
      small.mark = " "
    ),
    "]"
  )
}
#####################################
# function: format truncated ellipses
# Usage (in-line): `r tbi`
# Usage (console): tbi
#####################################
fmt_trunc_ellip <- function(
  x,
  digits = 7,
  ellip = "...",
  tol = 1e-12,
  trim_zeros_if_exact = TRUE
) {
  out <- rep(NA_character_, length(x))
  ok <- is.finite(x)
  scale <- 10^digits
  xt <- trunc(x[ok] * scale) / scale
  s <- formatC(xt, format = "f", digits = digits)
  add <- abs(x[ok] - xt) > tol * pmax(1, abs(x[ok]))
  if (trim_zeros_if_exact) {
    s_trim <- sub("0+$", "", s) # drop trailing zeros
    s_trim <- sub("\\.$", "", s_trim) # drop trailing decimal point
  } else {
    s_trim <- s
  }
  out[ok] <- ifelse(add, paste0(s, ellip), s_trim)
  out[is.na(x)] <- NA_character_
  out
}
#####################################
# function: function to format 6dp numbers with a space after the 3rd decimal
# digit
# Usage (in-line): TBI
# Usage (console): TBI
#####################################
format_spaced_6dp <- function(x) {
  # Format to 6 decimal places
  s <- sprintf("%.6f", x)
  # Insert space between the first 3 and last 3 decimal digits
  sub("(\\.[0-9]{3})([0-9]{3})$", "\\1 \\2", s)
}
#####################################
# function: compact inline number formatter
# Usage (in-line): `r fmt(2.32320)`
fmt <- function(x, d = 2) {
  formatC(x, format = "f", digits = d, big.mark = ",")
}
```

# Coursework 1 {-}

# Shrinkage estimation for portfolio construction {#coursework1}

```{r a-data-load, include=FALSE}
no_companies <- 100
# read in data (stored in data folder)
ret_raw <- read.csv(                    
  "data/DA441_weekly_returns.csv",
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE, 
  check.names = FALSE
)
rf_raw <- read.csv("data/risk_free_rate_weekly.csv", stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
gics <- as.data.frame(read_excel("data/DA441_GICS_Sectors.xlsx"))
# format dates
ret_raw$Date <- as.Date(as.character(ret_raw$Date), format = "%Y%m%d") 
rf_raw$Date  <- as.Date(as.character(rf_raw$Date),  format = "%Y%m%d")
```

```{r a-data-prep, include=FALSE}
## (i) convert risk-free rate from percentage -> decimal  ----
rf_dec    <- rf_raw # make quick copy
rf_dec$RF <- rf_raw$RF / 100
# align risk free rate to the return dates by date
dat <- merge(ret_raw, rf_dec[, c("Date", "RF")], by = "Date")
dates <- dat$Date
# original tickers, kept for GICS
ticker_cols <- setdiff(colnames(ret_raw), "Date")
returns_all <- as.matrix(dat[, ticker_cols])
rf <- dat$RF
# gets our specific tickers
set.seed(group_num)
selected <- sample(ticker_cols, no_companies)
returns100 <- returns_all[, selected]

## (ii) Weekly excess returns: r_excess[t,i] = r[t,i] - rf[t] ----
# sweep() subtracts the rf vector across rows (clearer than returns100 - rf)
excess_returns <- sweep(returns100, 1, rf, FUN = "-")
```

```{r a-params-estimators, include=FALSE}
# rolling-window parameters
training_weeks  <- 156
testing_weeks   <- 4
step_weeks      <- 4
omega           <- 1      # risk aversion gamma = 1

# BE note: n_assets should always equal no_companies as data comes pre-cleaned
n_total   <- nrow(excess_returns)
n_windows <- floor((n_total - training_weeks - testing_weeks) / step_weeks) + 1
n_weeks   <- n_total
n_assets  <- ncol(excess_returns)
np_ratio  <- training_weeks / n_assets
cat("Total number of rolling windows:", n_windows, "\n")

# Mean-vector estimators
# Check this* (each takes a returns matrix: rows = weeks, cols = assets).
mean_estimators <- list(
  Sample = function(X) colMeans(X),
  StMSh = St_MSh_estimator,
  DMSh = D_MSh_estimator,
  OLSh = O_LSh_estimator,
  TLSh = T_LSh_estimator,
  Wang = Wang_estimator,
  BOP = BOP_estimator,
  Jorion = Jorion_estimator
)
# Mirroring paper ordering from Asimit et al. 2026, Table 3
# proposed estimators -> sample -> benchmarks
est_order <- c(
  "StMSh", "DMSh", "OLSh", "TLSh", "Sample", "Wang", "BOP", "Jorion"
)
stopifnot(setequal(est_order, names(mean_estimators)))
# Single covariance estimator for the whole analysis
cov_fun <- cov1Para

# annualised Sharpe ratio from a weekly excess-return series
# > (mean/sd) * sqrt(52).
# > catches if assets in 4-week window have zero volatility
# > would get `Inf` or `NaN` -> this changes these values to NA()
ann_sharpe <- function(r, scale = 52) {
  r <- as.numeric(r)
  s <- sd(r)
  if (is.na(s) || s == 0) {
    return(NA_real_)
  }
  (mean(r) / s) * sqrt(scale)
}
```

```{r a-cov-diagnostic, include=TRUE}
# conditioning diagnostic
# cov1Para vs sample covariance
# mean estimator and porfolio construction held constant & just sigma changes

run_diag <- function(X, cov_estimator, mean_estimators,
                     training_weeks, testing_weeks, step_weeks) {
  n_windows <- floor((nrow(X) - training_weeks - testing_weeks) / step_weeks) + 1
  conds <- numeric(n_windows)
  Wl <- vector("list", n_windows)
  Rl <- vector("list", n_windows)
  for (k in seq_len(n_windows)) {
    tr_start <- 1 + (k - 1) * step_weeks
    tr_end <- tr_start + training_weeks - 1
    te_start <- tr_end + 1
    te_end <- te_start + testing_weeks - 1
    train_X <- X[tr_start:tr_end, , drop = FALSE]
    test_X <- X[te_start:te_end, , drop = FALSE]

    Sigma <- cov_estimator(train_X)
    ev <- eigen(Sigma, symmetric = TRUE, only.values = TRUE)$values
    conds[k] <- max(ev) / min(ev)
    Sigma_inv <- solve(Sigma)

    W <- sapply(mean_estimators, function(f) {
      z <- as.vector(Sigma_inv %*% f(train_X))
      z / sum(z) # tangency weights
    })
    colnames(W) <- names(mean_estimators)
    Wl[[k]] <- W
    Rl[[k]] <- test_X %*% W
  }
  gross <- rowMeans(sapply(Wl, function(W) colSums(abs(W))))
  turn <- rowMeans(sapply(2:n_windows, function(k) colSums(abs(Wl[[k]] - Wl[[k - 1]]))))
  oos <- apply(do.call(rbind, Rl), 2, ann_sharpe)
  list(
    cond_mean = mean(conds), cond_med = median(conds),
    tab = data.frame(
      GrossLev = gross, Turnover = turn, OOS_Sharpe = oos,
      row.names = names(mean_estimators)
    )
  )
}
diag_shrink <- run_diag(
  excess_returns, cov1Para, mean_estimators,
  training_weeks, testing_weeks, step_weeks
)
diag_sample <- run_diag(
  excess_returns, function(X) cov(X), mean_estimators,
  training_weeks, testing_weeks, step_weeks
)

cond_mean_shrink <- diag_shrink$cond_mean
cond_mean_sample <- diag_sample$cond_mean
cond_med_shrink <- diag_shrink$cond_med
cond_med_sample <- diag_sample$cond_med
cond_ratio_mean <- cond_mean_sample / cond_mean_shrink

cmp <- merge(diag_shrink$tab, diag_sample$tab,
  by = "row.names",
  suffixes = c("_cov1Para", "_sample")
)
rownames(cmp) <- cmp$Row.names
cmp$Row.names <- NULL
# cond_tab <- cmp[names(mean_estimators), ]
cond_tab <- cmp[est_order, ]
```

```{r a-rolling, include=FALSE}
# build mean variance and tangency portfolio weights per estimator (& EWP)
# > Score each by annualised Sharpe ratio over 4 week test window
# > Then roll forward across sample (described in labs)
eval_window <- function(train_X, test_X, cov_fun, mean_estimators, omega = 1) {
  Sigma_inv <- solve(cov_fun(train_X))
  out <- numeric(0)
  for (nm in names(mean_estimators)) {
    mu <- mean_estimators[[nm]](train_X)
    # Sigma^{-1} mu
    z <- as.vector(Sigma_inv %*% mu)
    # mean-variance (gamma = 1) <-> un-normalised
    w_mv <- (1 / omega) * z
    # tangency -> weights sum to 1
    w_tp <- z / sum(z)
    out[paste0("MV_", nm)] <- ann_sharpe(test_X %*% w_mv)
    out[paste0("TP_", nm)] <- ann_sharpe(test_X %*% w_tp)
  }
  w_ew <- rep(1 / ncol(train_X), ncol(train_X))
  # equal-weight benchmark
  out["EWP"] <- ann_sharpe(test_X %*% w_ew)
  out
}

sr_list <- vector("list", n_windows)
test_start_dates <- as.Date(rep(NA, n_windows))
for (k in seq_len(n_windows)) {
  tr_start <- 1 + (k - 1) * step_weeks
  tr_end <- tr_start + training_weeks - 1
  te_start <- tr_end + 1
  te_end <- te_start + testing_weeks - 1
  train_X <- excess_returns[tr_start:tr_end, , drop = FALSE]
  test_X <- excess_returns[te_start:te_end, , drop = FALSE]
  sr_list[[k]] <- eval_window(train_X, test_X,
    cov_fun         = cov_fun,
    mean_estimators = mean_estimators,
    omega           = omega
  )
  test_start_dates[k] <- dates[te_start]
}
sr_mat <- do.call(rbind, sr_list)
sr_df <- data.frame(TestStart = test_start_dates, sr_mat, check.names = FALSE)
```

```{r a-summaries-metrics, include=FALSE}
# Sub-period masks (assigned by test-window start date)
fc_lo <- as.Date("2008-10-06")
fc_hi <- as.Date("2011-08-03") # Financial Crisis
pd_lo <- as.Date("2020-02-26")
pd_hi <- as.Date("2023-12-29") # Pandemic

masks <- list(
  Full            = rep(TRUE, nrow(sr_df)),
  FinancialCrisis = sr_df$TestStart >= fc_lo & sr_df$TestStart <= fc_hi,
  Pandemic        = sr_df$TestStart >= pd_lo & sr_df$TestStart <= pd_hi
)
n_full <- sum(masks$Full)
n_fc <- sum(masks$FinancialCrisis)
n_pd <- sum(masks$Pandemic)

strategy_cols <- setdiff(colnames(sr_df), "TestStart")
summarise_period <- function(df, mask, cols) {
  sub <- df[mask, cols, drop = FALSE]
  data.frame(
    Mean = sapply(sub, mean, na.rm = TRUE),
    SD = sapply(sub, sd, na.rm = TRUE)
  )
}
summary_full <- summarise_period(sr_df, masks$Full, strategy_cols)
summary_fc <- summarise_period(sr_df, masks$FinancialCrisis, strategy_cols)
summary_pd <- summarise_period(sr_df, masks$Pandemic, strategy_cols)

cat(
  "Windows -> Full:",
  sum(masks$Full),
  "| Financial Crisis:",
  sum(masks$FinancialCrisis),
  "| Pandemic:",
  sum(masks$Pandemic),
  "\n"
)

summary_full$Strategy <- rownames(summary_full)
summary_fc$Strategy <- rownames(summary_fc)
summary_pd$Strategy <- rownames(summary_pd)
combined_summary <- summary_full |>
  dplyr::left_join(summary_fc, by = "Strategy", suffix = c("_full", "_fc")) |>
  dplyr::left_join(summary_pd, by = "Strategy") |>
  dplyr::rename(Mean_pd = Mean, SD_pd = SD) |>
  dplyr::relocate("Strategy")

row_order_hl <- c("EWP", paste0("MV_", est_order), paste0("TP_", est_order))
combined_summary <- combined_summary[
  match(row_order_hl, combined_summary$Strategy),
]

# per window mean-variance vs tagency portfolio equality (up to sign)
# & St-MSh == Sample check
mv <- as.matrix(sr_df[, grep("^MV_", names(sr_df))])
tp <- as.matrix(sr_df[, grep("^TP_", names(sr_df))])
mvtp_maxdiff <- max(abs(abs(mv) - abs(tp)), na.rm = TRUE)
stm_check <- isTRUE(all.equal(sr_df$MV_Sample, sr_df$MV_StMSh))

# ========================================================================== #
# scale sensitive out of sample metrics
# -> created as companion to the headline table
oos_metrics <- function(X, mean_estimators, cov_fun,
                        training_weeks, testing_weeks, step_weeks, omega = 1) {
  n_windows <- floor((nrow(X) - training_weeks - testing_weeks) / step_weeks) + 1
  est_names <- names(mean_estimators)
  strat <- c(paste0("MV_", est_names), paste0("TP_", est_names), "EWP")
  p <- ncol(X)

  # [1] Assets (p), [2] Strategy Name, [3] Rolling Window Step
  W_arr <- array(NA_real_,
    dim = c(p, length(strat), n_windows),
    dimnames = list(NULL, strat, NULL)
  )
  R_arr <- array(NA_real_,
    dim = c(testing_weeks, length(strat), n_windows),
    dimnames = list(NULL, strat, NULL)
  )
  sumz <- matrix(NA_real_, n_windows, length(est_names),
    dimnames = list(NULL, est_names)
  )
  for (k in seq_len(n_windows)) {
    tr_start <- 1 + (k - 1) * step_weeks
    tr_end <- tr_start + training_weeks - 1
    te_start <- tr_end + 1
    te_end <- te_start + testing_weeks - 1
    train_X <- X[tr_start:tr_end, , drop = FALSE]
    test_X <- X[te_start:te_end, , drop = FALSE]
    Sigma_inv <- solve(cov_fun(train_X))

    # Z = \Sigma^{-1} \mu
    Z <- sapply(mean_estimators, function(f) as.vector(Sigma_inv %*% f(train_X))) # p x 8
    sumz[k, ] <- colSums(Z)

    # W_mv = Z / \omega  |  W_tp = Z / (\mathbf{1}^T Z)
    W <- cbind(
      Z / omega, # MV (gamma = 1), unnormalised
      sweep(Z, 2, colSums(Z), "/"), # TP, weights sum to 1
      rep(1 / p, p)
    ) # EWP
    colnames(W) <- strat
    W_arr[, , k] <- W
    R_arr[, , k] <- test_X %*% W
  }
  list(W = W_arr, R = R_arr, sumz = sumz, strat = strat)
}
summarise_oos <- function(res, mask = rep(TRUE, dim(res$R)[3]), omega = 1, w_stat = mean) {
  W <- res$W[, , mask, drop = FALSE]
  R <- apply(res$R[, , mask, drop = FALSE], 2, c) # stitched weekly series
  ann_ret <- 52 * colMeans(R)
  ann_vol <- sqrt(52) * apply(R, 2, sd)
  sharpe <- apply(R, 2, ann_sharpe)
  ce <- ann_ret - (omega / 2) * ann_vol^2 # realised certainty-equivalent
  gross <- apply(apply(abs(W), c(2, 3), sum), 1, w_stat)
  maxw <- apply(apply(abs(W), c(2, 3), max), 1, w_stat)
  nW <- dim(W)[3]
  turn <- if (nW > 1) {
    dW <- abs(W[, , -1, drop = FALSE] - W[, , -nW, drop = FALSE])
    apply(apply(dW, c(2, 3), sum), 1, w_stat)
  } else {
    rep(NA_real_, length(res$strat))
  }
  est_names <- colnames(res$sumz)
  flips <- setNames(rep(NA_real_, length(res$strat)), res$strat)
  flips[paste0("TP_", est_names)] <- colSums(res$sumz[mask, , drop = FALSE] < 0)
  data.frame(
    AnnExRet = ann_ret, AnnVol = ann_vol, Sharpe = sharpe, CE = ce,
    GrossLev = gross, MaxWt = maxw, Turnover = turn,
    FlipWin = flips[res$strat], row.names = res$strat
  )
}
res_metrics <- oos_metrics(excess_returns, mean_estimators, cov_fun,
  training_weeks, testing_weeks, step_weeks,
  omega = omega
)
tab_full <- summarise_oos(res_metrics, masks$Full, omega = omega)
row_order <- c("EWP", paste0("MV_", est_order), paste0("TP_", est_order))
tab_full  <- tab_full[row_order, ]

# ========================================================================== #
# Debugging / tracability check: 1'Sigma^{-1}mu ~ 0
# > If the sum of unnormalised weights approaches zero the tangency portfolio
# > weights will explode to infinity (^ massive leverage).
# > This gets the worst-case window for the DMSh estimator to explore later
k_bad <- which.min(abs(res_metrics$sumz[, "DMSh"]))
sumz_bad <- res_metrics$sumz[k_bad, "DMSh"]
lev_bad <- sum(abs(res_metrics$W[, "TP_DMSh", k_bad]))
date_bad <- sr_df$TestStart[k_bad]
min_sumz <- apply(abs(res_metrics$sumz), 2, min)

# ========================================================================== #
# GICS (Global Industry Classification Standard) sector composition of
# sampled companies
id_col <- "DA441_column_name"
sector_col <- "GICS Sector Name"

sel_gics <- gics[gics[[id_col]] %in% selected, ]
sector_tab <- as.data.frame(table(sel_gics[[sector_col]]))
nrow(sel_gics) # check: should be 100 (Y)

colnames(sector_tab) <- c("Sector", "Count")
sector_tab <- sector_tab[order(-sector_tab$Count), ]

# BE note: quick calc to get number and % of GICS companies used in part A
# writeup
get_sec <- function(s) {
  v <- sector_tab$Count[sector_tab$Sector == s]
  if (length(v)) v else 0L
}
n_fin <- get_sec("Financials")
n_it <- get_sec("Information Technology")
n_comms <- get_sec("Communication Services")
pct_it_comms <- round(100 * (n_it + n_comms) / sum(sector_tab$Count))
```

```{r a-inout-compute, include=FALSE}
# In-sample vs out-of-sample tangency Sharpe, per window per estimator
in_out_sample <- function(X, dates, mean_estimators, cov_fun,
                          training_weeks, testing_weeks, step_weeks) {
  n_windows <- floor((nrow(X) - training_weeks - testing_weeks) / step_weeks) + 1
  rows <- list()
  i <- 1
  for (k in seq_len(n_windows)) {
    tr_start <- 1 + (k - 1) * step_weeks
    tr_end <- tr_start + training_weeks - 1
    te_start <- tr_end + 1
    te_end <- te_start + testing_weeks - 1
    train_X <- X[tr_start:tr_end, , drop = FALSE]
    test_X <- X[te_start:te_end, , drop = FALSE]
    Sigma_inv <- solve(cov_fun(train_X))
    for (nm in names(mean_estimators)) {
      z <- as.vector(Sigma_inv %*% mean_estimators[[nm]](train_X))
      w <- z / sum(z)
      rows[[i]] <- data.frame(
        window = k, Date = dates[te_start], estimator = nm,
        denom = sum(z),
        IS = ann_sharpe(train_X %*% w),
        OOS = ann_sharpe(test_X %*% w)
      )
      i <- i + 1
    }
  }
  out <- do.call(rbind, rows)
  out$flip <- out$denom < 0
  out
}
diag_df <- in_out_sample(
  excess_returns, dates, mean_estimators, cov_fun,
  training_weeks, testing_weeks, step_weeks
)
diag_df$estimator <- factor(diag_df$estimator, levels = est_order)
inout_summary <- diag_df |>
  dplyr::group_by(estimator) |>
  dplyr::summarise(
    `Windows flipped (%)` = mean(flip) * 100,
    `OOS Sharpe (flipped)` = mean(OOS[flip], na.rm = TRUE),
    `OOS Sharpe (not flipped)` = mean(OOS[!flip], na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  dplyr::arrange(match(estimator, est_order))
```

---

## Data preprocessing {#a1}

The risk-free rate was first converted from a percentage to a decimal (i) before
weekly excess returns were computed (ii) using Eq. \@ref(eq:excess-returns).
\begin{equation}
r^{\text{excess}}_{i,t} = r_{i,t} - r_{f,t}
(\#eq:excess-returns)
\end{equation}
The risk-free rate was aligned to the return dates, and the excess returns were
calculated using the `sweep()` function to subtract the risk-free vector across
the rows. The resulting excess return matrix covers `r n_weeks` weekly
observations for `r n_assets` companies, from `r as.character(min(dates))` to 
`r as.character(max(dates))`. In **R**, both of these steps were implemented
using:

```{r a-data-prep-show, ref.label="a-data-prep", echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
```

## Selection of estimation models {#a2}

Portfolios were constructed using all eight mean-vector estimators: the sample 
mean, the four distribution-free shrinkage estimators of @Asimit15062026 
(`St-MSh`, `D-MSh`, `O-LSh` and `T-LSh`), and the three established benchmarks 
(`Wang`, `BOP` and `Jorion`). Please see Appendix
\@ref(apx-method-mean-vec-estimators) for formal definitions of each estimator.

Every mean estimator is paired with the one-parameter linear shrinkage estimator of @Ledoit2004365, `cov1Para`, rather than
the sample covariance. `cov1Para` pulls the sample covariance $S$ toward a
diagonal matrix $\mu I$ whose common variance
$\mu = \tfrac{1}{p}\operatorname{tr}(S)$ is the average sample variance. 
The strength of the pull is set automatically, with no tuning parameter, to minimise the covariance's mean-squared error (Appendix \@ref(apx-method-mean-vec-estimators)).
The result is always positive-definite and well-conditioned. It is also
distribution-free, needing only i.i.d. observations with finite fourth moments,
the same assumptions that underlie the mean estimators of @Asimit15062026. The
whole pipeline therefore rests on one consistent set of assumptions.

The issue with the sample covariance is conditioning. Each
training window gives $n = `r training_weeks`$ weekly observations on
$p = `r n_assets`$ assets, a ratio of $n/p \approx `r fmt(np_ratio, 2)`$.
Because $n > p$ the sample covariance can be inverted, but it is
ill-conditioned. Its smallest eigenvalues are biased toward zero, so inverting
the covariance amplifies the directions estimated least reliably
[@Ledoit2004365], producing extreme, unstable weights whose errors accumulate
out of sample.

Both the problem and the fix are visible directly in our data. Averaged across
the `r n_windows` windows (described in more detail in \@ref(a3)), the condition
number of the sample covariance is
`r fmt(cond_mean_sample, 0)` compared to `r fmt(cond_mean_shrink, 0)` under
`cov1Para` - a `r round(cond_ratio_mean)`-fold improvement (medians
`r fmt(cond_med_sample, 0)` vs `r fmt(cond_med_shrink, 0)`). 
With the mean estimators and portfolio rule held fixed, the better conditioning 
feeds straight into implementability. For the `O-LSh` tangency portfolio, 
average gross leverage falls from 
`r fmt(cond_tab["OLSh", "GrossLev_sample"], 1)` to
`r fmt(cond_tab["OLSh", "GrossLev_cov1Para"], 1)` and monthly turnover from
`r fmt(cond_tab["OLSh", "Turnover_sample"], 1)` to
`r fmt(cond_tab["OLSh", "Turnover_cov1Para"], 1)`, with comparable reductions
for every estimator (Appendix \@ref(apx-cond)). 

## Portfolio construction and rolling-window setup {#a3}

For each window and each mean estimator, two portfolios are built: a
mean-variance portfolio with risk aversion $\gamma = 1$, giving the unnormalised
weights $w_{MV}$, and a tangency portfolio, the same vector rescaled to sum to
one, $w_{TP}$,
$$
w_{MV} = \Sigma^{-1}\hat\mu \, ,
\qquad
w_{TP} = \frac{\Sigma^{-1}\hat\mu}{\mathbf 1^\top\Sigma^{-1}\hat\mu}
$$
The equal-weight portfolio (EWP) is included as a
benchmark. In our code, $\gamma$ is denoted `omega`. Throughout, $\Sigma$ is the
`cov1Para` estimate selected in \@ref(a2), and $\hat\mu$ is estimated on the
training window only, so no test-window information enters the weights.

The rolling structure uses a 156-week (3-year) training window, a 4-week
(1-month) testing window, and a 4-week step. Because the step equals the testing
length, successive test windows are contiguous and non-overlapping. Over the 
`r n_weeks`-week sample this gives 
$$
\lfloor(`r n_weeks` - 156 - 4)/4\rfloor + 1 = `r n_windows`
$$
out-of-sample windows, leaving the final 
`r n_weeks - 156 - testing_weeks * n_windows` weeks uncovered by any test window. The implementation of this rolling structure in **R** was:

```{=latex}
\newpage
```

```{r rolling-show, ref.label="a-rolling", echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
```


## Portfolio performance {#a4}

For every test window and every portfolio the annualised Sharpe ratio was
calculated by taking the mean of the four weekly out-of-sample excess returns,
dividing by their standard deviation and multiplying by $\sqrt{52}$. Each window
is assigned to a market environment by its test-window start date.
Of the `r n_windows` windows, `r n_fc` fall in the financial-crisis sub-period
(`r format(fc_lo, "%d/%m/%Y")`-`r format(fc_hi, "%d/%m/%Y")`) and `r n_pd` in
the pandemic sub-period 
(`r format(pd_lo, "%d/%m/%Y")`-`r format(pd_hi, "%d/%m/%Y")`).
These boundary dates are structural breakpoints identified by the Bai-Perron
test on the S&P500 return series and the sub-periods correspond to statistically
identified market regimes rather than arbitrary calendar choices. 

Table \@ref(tab:a-headline-sharpe) shows the mean and standard deviation of the
per-window Sharpe ratios over the full period and the two sub-periods. Each
per-window Sharpe ratio is estimated from only four weekly returns, so it is
inherently noisy. Cross-window standard deviations range from
approximately `r fmt(min(summary_full$SD), 1)` to 
`r fmt(max(summary_full$SD), 1)` over the full period, in 
every case several times the magnitude of the corresponding mean
(approximately `r fmt(min(summary_full$Mean), 2)` to 
`r fmt(max(summary_full$Mean), 2)`). This dispersion is a property of the
per-window definition. A companion summary for a Sharpe ratio computed on the
stitched out-of-sample series is described in Appendix \@ref(apx-scale).

Note that the Sample and `St-MSh` rows in Table \@ref(tab:a-headline-sharpe) are
identical in every period. This is a structural property of the Sharpe ratio. `St-MSh` scales the sample mean by a positive constant
that the Sharpe ratio cannot detect. This is explored further in Appendix
\@ref(apx-stmsh-vs-sample) (the same identity is also present in Table 3 of
@Asimit15062026).

```{r a-headline-sharpe}
hl <- combined_summary
hl$Strategy <- sub("^(MV|TP)_", "", hl$Strategy)
n_est <- length(est_order)

kable(hl,
  digits = 4, booktabs = TRUE, row.names = FALSE,
  caption = "Mean and standard deviation of the per-window annualised Sharpe ratios. The best mean and standard deviation in each environment are highlighted. Because the Sharpe ratio is invariant to positive rescaling of the weights, St-MSh reproduces the Sample rows exactly, and the mean-variance and tangency portfolios coincide except in net-short windows where the sign flips.",
  linesep = "",
  col.names = c("Strategy", "Mean", "SD", "Mean", "SD", "Mean", "SD")
) |>
  kable_styling(
    full_width = FALSE, position = "center", 
    latex_options = "hold_position"
  ) |>
  add_header_above(c(" " = 1, "Full period" = 2, "Financial crisis" = 2, "Pandemic" = 2)) |>
  pack_rows(index = c(
    "Benchmark" = 1,
    "Mean-variance portfolio" = n_est,
    "Tangency portfolio" = n_est
  )) |>
  # adds latex spacing (just for pdf)
  row_spec(c(5, 13), extra_latex_after = "\\addlinespace") |>
  column_spec(2,
    bold = combined_summary$Mean_full == max(combined_summary$Mean_full, na.rm = TRUE),
    color = ifelse(combined_summary$Mean_full == max(combined_summary$Mean_full, na.rm = TRUE), "#1b9e77", "black")
  ) |>
  column_spec(4,
    bold = combined_summary$Mean_fc == max(combined_summary$Mean_fc, na.rm = TRUE),
    color = ifelse(combined_summary$Mean_fc == max(combined_summary$Mean_fc, na.rm = TRUE), "#1b9e77", "black")
  ) |>
  column_spec(6,
    bold = combined_summary$Mean_pd == max(combined_summary$Mean_pd, na.rm = TRUE),
    color = ifelse(combined_summary$Mean_pd == max(combined_summary$Mean_pd, na.rm = TRUE), "#1b9e77", "black")
  ) |>
  column_spec(3,
    bold = combined_summary$SD_full == min(combined_summary$SD_full, na.rm = TRUE),
    color = ifelse(combined_summary$SD_full == min(combined_summary$SD_full, na.rm = TRUE), "#1b9e77", "black")
  ) |>
  column_spec(5,
    bold = combined_summary$SD_fc == min(combined_summary$SD_fc, na.rm = TRUE),
    color = ifelse(combined_summary$SD_fc == min(combined_summary$SD_fc, na.rm = TRUE), "#1b9e77", "black")
  ) |>
  column_spec(7,
    bold = combined_summary$SD_pd == min(combined_summary$SD_pd, na.rm = TRUE),
    color = ifelse(combined_summary$SD_pd == min(combined_summary$SD_pd, na.rm = TRUE), "#1b9e77", "black")
  )
```


## Interpretation of results and final report {#a5}

<!-- BE note: was quite brutal here to get this down -->
<!-- - Aimed for ~1/2 to 1 page max -->

Optimised portfolios are only as reliable as the expected returns they are built
on, and those estimates carry substantial estimation error. This report tests
whether shrinkage methods designed to temper that noise deliver better
risk-adjusted returns than the classical sample mean for an investor rebalancing
monthly over two decades [@Asimit15062026]. Using weekly excess returns for
`r no_companies` S&P 500 companies 
(`r format(min(dates), "%B %Y")`-`r format(max(dates), "%B %Y")`), 
eight ways of estimating expected returns were compared, each paired with the
`cov1Para` covariance estimator [@Ledoit2004365] and benchmarked against an
equal-weight (1/N) portfolio. Every method was evaluated with a rolling window
that mirrors live practice, fiting on the previous three years, testing on the
next unseen month, then advancing, giving `r n_windows` out-of-sample tests with
no look-ahead bias. Performance was summarised by the annualised Sharpe ratio
over the full period, the 2008 financial crisis, and the COVID-19 pandemic
(Table \@ref(tab:a-headline-sharpe)).

The mean-variance and tangency portfolios hold the same positions at different
scales (tangency rescales the weights to sum to one) so within any window
their Sharpe ratios are identical bar a possible sign flip (the per-window
magnitudes agree to within `r formatC(mvtp_maxdiff, format = "e", digits = 1)`).
The two coincide in every window where the portfolio is not net-short, which
covers almost the entire pandemic. Over other periods the sign flip lifts the
tangency full-period mean above mean-variance for the stronger estimators
(`TP_OLSh`
`r fmt(summary_full["TP_OLSh", "Mean"], 4)` versus `MV_OLSh`
`r fmt(summary_full["MV_OLSh", "Mean"], 4)`). The economically meaningful
difference is cost, not Sharpe ratio. Because tangency weights sum to one,
leverage and turnover are far lower (for `O-LSh`, average leverage falls from
`r fmt(tab_full["MV_OLSh", "GrossLev"], 1)` to
`r fmt(tab_full["TP_OLSh", "GrossLev"], 1)`), making tangency preferable in
practice. The exception is when the normaliser nears zero and weights explode
(`r format(date_bad, "%d %B %Y")` drives `TP_DMSh` leverage to roughly
`r fmt(lev_bad, 0)`; Appendix \@ref(apx-pathology)).

Across estimators, the standout result is how hard the naïve benchmark is to
beat. The equal-weight portfolio posts the highest full-period Sharpe
(`r fmt(summary_full["EWP", "Mean"], 4)`) and lowest full-period dispersion,
overtaken only once, by `TP_OLSh` in the financial crisis
(`r fmt(summary_fc["TP_OLSh", "Mean"], 4)` versus
`r fmt(summary_fc["EWP", "Mean"], 4)`), consistent with the 1/N result of
@DeMiguel2009. Among optimised methods only mean shrinkage keeps up. Under the tangency construction, `O-LSh` and `D-MSh` roughly double the sample mean's full-period Sharpe ratio 
(`r fmt(summary_full["TP_OLSh", "Mean"], 4)` and
`r fmt(summary_full["TP_DMSh", "Mean"], 4)` versus 
`r fmt(summary_full["TP_Sample", "Mean"], 4)`), with `D-MSh` the most consistent across regimes and the best optimised pandemic performer 
(`r fmt(summary_pd["TP_DMSh", "Mean"], 4)`). 
The raw sample mean turns negative in the financial crisis 
(`r fmt(summary_fc["MV_Sample", "Mean"], 4)`) through error-maximisation,
`St-MSh` reproduces the sample rows exactly (Appendix
\@ref(apx-stmsh-vs-sample)), `Jorion` is too volatile to trust
(SD `r fmt(summary_full["MV_Jorion", "SD"], 2)`, rising to
`r fmt(summary_pd["TP_Jorion", "SD"], 2)` in the pandemic), and `BOP` is the
weakest overall, with the most net-short flips (`r tab_full["TP_BOP",
"FlipWin"]` windows). The uniformly weak pandemic figures are partly due to our
group's sector draw (Figure \@ref(fig:gics-fig); full company list in Appendix
\@ref(apx-stocks)): Financials is the largest single exposure (`r n_fin` of
100), and while Information Technology sits mid-pack (`r n_it`), the tech-led
sectors that drove the post-2020 rally are thin as a bloc with IT and
Communication Services (`r n_comms`) together only accounting for 
`r pct_it_comms`% of the universe. No estimator can manufacture exposure that 
isn't there and an equal-weighted portfolio sidesteps this problem by never guessing. 
For our 100 stocks, `O-LSh` and `D-MSh` are worth using over the raw sample
mean, but once leverage and turnover are counted, a plain equal-weighted
portfolio remains a strong, cheap benchmark the optimisers struggle to beat out
of sample (Appendix \@ref(apx-scale)).


```{r gics-table, eval=FALSE}
# BE note: not loaded as opted for the bar chart below instead
# Kept for posterity
kable(sector_tab,
  row.names = FALSE, booktabs = TRUE,
  caption = "GICS sector composition of our 100 sampled companies (Group 12)."
) |>
  kable_styling(full_width = FALSE, position = "center")
```

```{r gics-fig, fig.cap="GICS sector composition of the 100 sampled companies (Group 12), shown as a horizontal bar chart with sectors ordered by company count. Financials is the largest exposure, while Communication Services and the wider tech-led bloc are comparatively underweight.", fig.width=5.5, fig.height=2.8}
ggplot(sector_tab, aes(x = Count, y = reorder(Sector, Count))) +
  geom_col(fill = "#0072B2") +
  labs(x = "Companies", y = NULL) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 15), breaks = seq(0, 15, by = 5)) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )
```

```{=latex}
\newpage
```

# Coursework 2 {-}

# Shrinkage Generalised Linear Models for Insurance Claim Severity Estimation {#coursework2}

```{=latex}
% Custom shortened header to avoid overlap
\sectionmark{Shrinkage GLMs for Insurance Claim Severity Estimation}
```


```{r b-setup, include=FALSE}
# BE note: seed already set above.
#          > also moved libraries up to pre-loading shared with part A

# shared fitting config
n_folds <- 5
control_list <- list(maxit = 250, epsilon = 1e-6, trace = FALSE)
family_type <- Gamma(link = "log")

# Model formula and the columns that get standardised. Defined here -> used by:
# > the CV engine
# > the B3 in-sample fit
# > appendix sensitivity checks
glm_formula <- Severity ~ BonusMalus_Log + VehPower_Log + Density_Log +
  DrivAge_Sp1 + DrivAge_Sp2 + DrivAge_Sp3 +
  VehAge_Sp1 + VehAge_Sp2 + VehAge_Sp3 +
  VehGas + TE_Region + TE_Area + TE_VehBrand
scale_cols <- c(
  "BonusMalus_Log", "VehPower_Log", "Density_Log",
  "DrivAge_Sp1", "DrivAge_Sp2", "DrivAge_Sp3",
  "VehAge_Sp1", "VehAge_Sp2", "VehAge_Sp3",
  "TE_Region", "TE_Area", "TE_VehBrand"
)

# put model names in order
model_names <- c("GLM2", "RR", "EN", "QIS", "LW", "SR", "GSR", "St", "DSh")
```

```{r b-data-prep, include=FALSE}
# Read in data (stored in relative data/ folder)
freq <- read.csv("data/freMTPL2freq.csv")
sev <- read.csv("data/freMTPL2sev.csv")
# Aggregate severity by policy ID (& merge with frequency features)
sev_agg <- aggregate(ClaimAmount ~ IDpol, data = sev, FUN = sum)
data <- merge(freq, sev_agg, by = "IDpol")
# Filter for exactly 1 claim and positive claim amount
data_sev <- data[data$ClaimNb == 1 & data$ClaimAmount > 0, ]

# Group seed set before the single random draw of 1,000 rows just to be safe
# (avoiding part A rng leakage)
set.seed(group_num)
# Randomly sample 1000 rows
data_sev <- data_sev[sample(nrow(data_sev), 1000), ]
# for generality (= ClaimAmount)
data_sev$Severity <- data_sev$ClaimAmount / data_sev$ClaimNb

# Squash heavy tails and bound implausible values.
# > defaults:
#   VehPower capped at 9
#   BonusMalus at 150
# sensitivity to both caps is tested in the appendix (apx-b-caps)
data_sev$VehPower_Log <- log(pmax(pmin(data_sev$VehPower, 9), 1))
data_sev$Density_Log <- log(pmax(data_sev$Density, 1))
data_sev$BonusMalus_Log <- log(pmin(data_sev$BonusMalus, 150))
# Quick check -> should be 1000
n_pol <- nrow(data_sev)
```

```{r b-metric-helpers, include=FALSE}
# OOS evaluation metrics ====
# BE note: removed return function (slightly faster if used in loop)
calc_rmse_win <- function(actual, predicted, cap_val) {
  sqrt(mean((pmin(actual, cap_val) - pmin(predicted, cap_val))^2))
}
calc_mae_win <- function(actual, predicted, cap_val) {
  mean(abs(pmin(actual, cap_val) - pmin(predicted, cap_val)))
}

WeightedGini <- function(actual, weights, predicted) {
  df <- data.frame(actual = actual, weights = weights, predicted = predicted)
  df <- df[order(df$predicted, decreasing = TRUE), ]
  df$random <- cumsum(df$weights / sum(df$weights))
  totalPositive <- sum(df$actual * df$weights)
  df$cumPosFound <- cumsum(df$actual * df$weights)
  df$Lorentz <- df$cumPosFound / totalPositive
  n <- nrow(df)
  gini <- sum(df$Lorentz[-n] * df$random[-1]) - sum(df$Lorentz[-1] * df$random[-n])
  # implicity return
  gini
}
NormalisedWeightedGini <- function(actual, weights, predicted) {
  model_gini <- WeightedGini(actual, weights, predicted)
  max_gini <- WeightedGini(actual, weights, actual)
  # implicity return
  model_gini / max_gini
}
```

```{r b-feature-engine, include=FALSE}
# Leakage-safe feature construction ====
# Used for appendix
# function encapsulating the pipeline used inside every CV fold
#   splines fitted on training rows only
#   smoothed leave-one-out target encoding learned on training rows only
#   standardisation frozen at training means/sds.
# With test = NULL it prepares the full dataset for the in-sample analysis (B3) and for the appendix sensitivity checks, using the same construction.
build_features <- function(train, test = NULL,
                           veh_cap = 9, bm_cap = 150,
                           driv_df = 3, veh_df = 3,
                           k_smooth = 20, standardise = TRUE) {
  # reapply caps from the raw columns so the appendix can vary them
  for (d in list(quote(train), quote(test))) {
    dd <- eval(d)
    if (is.null(dd)) next
    dd$VehPower_Log <- log(pmax(pmin(dd$VehPower, veh_cap), 1))
    dd$Density_Log <- log(pmax(dd$Density, 1))
    dd$BonusMalus_Log <- log(pmin(dd$BonusMalus, bm_cap))
    if (identical(d, quote(train))) train <- dd else test <- dd
  }

  # Natural cubic splines fitted strictly on the training data
  driv_splines <- ns(train$DrivAge, df = driv_df)
  veh_splines <- ns(train$VehAge, df = veh_df)
  for (j in seq_len(driv_df)) train[[paste0("DrivAge_Sp", j)]] <- driv_splines[, j]
  for (j in seq_len(veh_df)) train[[paste0("VehAge_Sp", j)]] <- veh_splines[, j]
  if (!is.null(test)) {
    tdb <- predict(driv_splines, test$DrivAge)
    tvb <- predict(veh_splines, test$VehAge)
    for (j in seq_len(driv_df)) test[[paste0("DrivAge_Sp", j)]] <- tdb[, j]
    for (j in seq_len(veh_df)) test[[paste0("VehAge_Sp", j)]] <- tvb[, j]
  }

  # actuarial (credibility-smoothed) target encoding with leave-one-out on
  # the training rows, and the full training dictionary projected onto test
  te_cols <- c("Region", "Area", "VehBrand")
  global_mean_train <- sum(train$ClaimAmount) / sum(train$ClaimNb)
  for (col in te_cols) {
    group_amt <- tapply(train$ClaimAmount, train[[col]], sum)
    group_clm <- tapply(train$ClaimNb, train[[col]], sum)
    grp_idx <- as.character(train[[col]])
    loo_amt <- group_amt[grp_idx] - train$ClaimAmount
    loo_clm <- group_clm[grp_idx] - train$ClaimNb
    lambda_train <- loo_clm / (loo_clm + k_smooth)
    mu_train <- ifelse(loo_clm > 0, loo_amt / loo_clm, global_mean_train)
    train[[paste0("TE_", col)]] <-
      lambda_train * mu_train + (1 - lambda_train) * global_mean_train
    if (!is.null(test)) {
      full_lambda <- group_clm / (group_clm + k_smooth)
      full_mu <- group_amt / group_clm
      dict <- full_lambda * full_mu + (1 - full_lambda) * global_mean_train
      te <- dict[as.character(test[[col]])]
      te[is.na(te)] <- global_mean_train # unseen levels -> global baseline
      test[[paste0("TE_", col)]] <- te
    }
  }

  # Standardisation, frozen at training-data parameters
  # Not strictly required for glm2/savvyGLM/glmnet -> see (apx-b-scaling)
  # retained for posterity
  sc <- intersect(scale_cols, names(train))
  if (standardise) {
    tm <- colMeans(train[, sc])
    ts <- apply(train[, sc], 2, sd)
    ts[ts == 0] <- 1
    for (col in sc) {
      train[[col]] <- (train[[col]] - tm[col]) / ts[col]
      if (!is.null(test)) test[[col]] <- (test[[col]] - tm[col]) / ts[col]
    }
  }
  list(train = train, test = test)
}

# Single dispatcher: model name -> coefficient vector aligned to columns of X
# Used by B3 and the appendix so the best model is picked in one place
fit_model_coefs <- function(model, X, y, w) {
  if (model == "GLM2") {
    m <- glm.fit2(
      x = X, y = y, weights = w,
      family = family_type, control = control_list
    )
    m$coefficients
  } else if (model %in% c("RR", "EN")) {
    m <- cv.glmnet(X[, -1], y,
      weights = w, family = Gamma(link = "log"),
      alpha = ifelse(model == "RR", 0, 0.5),
      nlambda = 50, nfolds = 5
    )
    as.vector(coef(m, s = "lambda.min"))
  } else {
    m <- savvy_glm.fit2(
      x = X, y = y, weights = w, family = family_type,
      model_class = model, control = control_list
    )
    m$coefficients
  }
}
```

```{r b-cv-function, include=FALSE}
# Cross-validation engine (B1) ====
# BE note: cv_seed_base following tutorial code
cv_seed_base <- 123

run_single_iteration <- function(i) {
  repeat_idx <- floor((i - 1) / n_folds) + 1 # 1..100 across the 500 runs
  fold_idx   <- ((i - 1) %% n_folds) + 1     # cycles 1..5 within each repeat

  # Reshuffle FOLD LABELS only (repeat r uses seed cv_seed_base + r for all
  # five of its runs); the 1,000 sampled rows themselves are never redrawn.
  set.seed(cv_seed_base + repeat_idx)
  folds <- sample(rep(1:n_folds, length.out = nrow(data_sev)))
  test_idx <- which(folds == fold_idx)

  fe <- build_features(data_sev[-test_idx, ], data_sev[test_idx, ])
  train_data <- fe$train
  test_data  <- fe$test

  X_train <- model.matrix(glm_formula, train_data)
  X_test  <- model.matrix(glm_formula, test_data)
  y_train <- train_data$Severity; y_test <- test_data$Severity
  w_train <- train_data$ClaimNb;  w_test <- test_data$ClaimNb

  # Winsorisation caps learned from the TRAINING response only
  cap_99 <- quantile(y_train, probs = 0.99, na.rm = TRUE)
  cap_95 <- quantile(y_train, probs = 0.95, na.rm = TRUE)

  eval_model <- function(coefs) {
    if (all(is.na(coefs))) return(rep(NA_real_, 10))
    safe_coefs <- ifelse(is.na(coefs), 0, coefs)
    pred_test <- exp(as.numeric(X_test %*% safe_coefs))
    c(sqrt(mean((y_test - pred_test)^2)),
      mean(abs(y_test - pred_test)),
      calc_rmse_win(y_test, pred_test, cap_99),
      calc_rmse_win(y_test, pred_test, cap_95),
      calc_mae_win(y_test, pred_test, cap_99),
      calc_mae_win(y_test, pred_test, cap_95),
      WeightedGini(y_test, w_test, pred_test),
      NormalisedWeightedGini(y_test, w_test, pred_test),
      sum(w_test * Gamma()$dev.resids(y_test, pred_test, 1)),
      sum(y_test * w_test) / sum(pred_test * w_test))
  }

  # 1. GLM2 (baseline)
  t1 <- system.time({ m1 <- glm.fit2(x = X_train, y = y_train, weights = w_train,
                                     family = family_type, control = control_list) })
  res1 <- eval_model(m1$coefficients); iter1 <- m1$iter; conv1 <- m1$converged
  # 2. Ridge
  t2 <- system.time({ m2 <- cv.glmnet(X_train[, -1], y_train, weights = w_train,
                                      family = Gamma(link = "log"), alpha = 0,
                                      nlambda = 50, nfolds = 5) })
  res2 <- eval_model(as.vector(coef(m2, s = "lambda.min")))
  iter2 <- if (is.null(m2$glmnet.fit$npasses)) NA else m2$glmnet.fit$npasses[1]
  conv2 <- NA
  # 3. Elastic Net
  t3 <- system.time({ m3 <- cv.glmnet(X_train[, -1], y_train, weights = w_train,
                                      family = Gamma(link = "log"), alpha = 0.5,
                                      nlambda = 50, nfolds = 5) })
  res3 <- eval_model(as.vector(coef(m3, s = "lambda.min")))
  iter3 <- if (is.null(m3$glmnet.fit$npasses)) NA else m3$glmnet.fit$npasses[1]
  conv3 <- NA
  # 4.-9. Shrinkage GLMs (savvyGLM)
  savvy_classes <- c("St", "DSh", "SR", "GSR", "LW", "QIS")
  savvy_out <- lapply(savvy_classes, function(cl) {
    tt <- system.time({ m <- savvy_glm.fit2(x = X_train, y = y_train,
                                            weights = w_train,
                                            family = family_type,
                                            model_class = cl,
                                            control = control_list) })
    list(res = eval_model(m$coefficients), t = tt[3], it = m$iter,
         conv = isTRUE(m$converged))
  })
  names(savvy_out) <- savvy_classes

  ord <- c("GLM2", "RR", "EN", "QIS", "LW", "SR", "GSR", "St", "DSh")
  all_res <- rbind(res1, res2, res3,
                   savvy_out$QIS$res, savvy_out$LW$res, savvy_out$SR$res,
                   savvy_out$GSR$res, savvy_out$St$res, savvy_out$DSh$res)
  times <- c(t1[3], t2[3], t3[3], sapply(ord[4:9], function(cl) savvy_out[[cl]]$t))
  iters <- c(iter1, iter2, iter3, sapply(ord[4:9], function(cl) savvy_out[[cl]]$it))
  convs <- c(conv1, conv2, conv3, sapply(ord[4:9], function(cl) savvy_out[[cl]]$conv))

  df_metrics <- data.frame(Iteration = i, Model = ord,
             RMSE = all_res[, 1], MAE = all_res[, 2],
             RMSE_Win_99 = all_res[, 3], RMSE_Win_95 = all_res[, 4],
             MAE_Win_99 = all_res[, 5], MAE_Win_95 = all_res[, 6],
             Gini_Raw = all_res[, 7], Gini_Norm = all_res[, 8],
             Dev = all_res[, 9], AE_Ratio = all_res[, 10],
             CT = as.numeric(times), NoIt = as.numeric(iters),
             Converged = convs)

  df_metrics
}

```

```{r b1-loop, eval=FALSE, echo=FALSE}
# NOTE: the full 100 x 5 = 500-run loop takes ~20 minutes
# results are pre-computed once and cached to final_results.rds (loaded below)
n_repeats <- 100
n_iter <- n_repeats * n_folds
results_list <- vector("list", n_iter)
for (i in seq_len(n_iter)) {
  results_list[[i]] <- run_single_iteration(i)
  cat(sprintf("Finished iteration %d of %d.\n", i, n_iter))
}
final_results <- do.call(rbind, results_list)
saveRDS(final_results, "data/final_results.rds")
```

```{r b1-load, eval=TRUE, echo=FALSE}
# Load cached results & check the cache matches the final configuration
#   filter ClaimNb == 1
#   group seed 12
#   n = 1,000
#   500 runs
#   9 models
final_results <- readRDS("data/final_results.rds")
n_runs <- length(unique(final_results$Iteration))
stopifnot(
  n_pol == 1000,
  n_runs == 500,
  setequal(unique(final_results$Model), model_names)
)
```

```{r b1-summaries, include=FALSE}
# Raw and relative performance tables ====
metric_cols <- c(
  "RMSE", "MAE", "RMSE_Win_99", "RMSE_Win_95",
  "MAE_Win_99", "MAE_Win_95", "Dev",
  "Gini_Raw", "Gini_Norm", "AE_Ratio", "CT", "NoIt"
)

# Raw table: mean of each metric per model across all 500 runs
raw_tab <- aggregate(final_results[, metric_cols],
  by = list(Model = final_results$Model),
  FUN = mean, na.rm = TRUE
)
raw_tab <- raw_tab[match(model_names, raw_tab$Model), ]

# Relative table: mean of PER-RUN ratios vs the GLM2 value in the SAME run
# (ratio-of-averages would understate run-to-run variation; the brief asks
# for the average of ratios).
glm2_rows <- final_results[final_results$Model == "GLM2", ]
glm2_aligned <- glm2_rows[
  match(final_results$Iteration, glm2_rows$Iteration),
  metric_cols
]
rel_runs <- final_results
rel_runs[metric_cols] <- final_results[metric_cols] / glm2_aligned
rel_tab <- aggregate(rel_runs[, metric_cols],
  by = list(Model = rel_runs$Model),
  FUN = mean, na.rm = TRUE
)
rel_tab <- rel_tab[match(model_names, rel_tab$Model), ]

# Display copy for the kable. The per-run GLM2 Gini is small and frequently
# negative, so Gini ratios flip sign and explode
# Both Gini columns (and AE, a ratio of ratios) are judged from the raw table
# rel_tab itself is kept numeric for inline references
ratio_excluded <- c("Gini_Raw", "Gini_Norm", "AE_Ratio")
rel_tab_disp <- rel_tab
rel_tab_disp[ratio_excluded] <- NA_real_

# Direction of "better", per the brief, used for highlighting
metric_dir <- c(
  RMSE = "min", MAE = "min", RMSE_Win_99 = "min",
  RMSE_Win_95 = "min", MAE_Win_99 = "min", MAE_Win_95 = "min",
  Dev = "min", Gini_Raw = "max", Gini_Norm = "max",
  AE_Ratio = "one", CT = "min", NoIt = "min"
)
best_flag <- function(v, dir) {
  switch(dir,
    min = v == min(v, na.rm = TRUE),
    max = v == max(v, na.rm = TRUE),
    one = abs(v - 1) == min(abs(v - 1), na.rm = TRUE)
  )
}

# Convergence summary for the appendix (NA for glmnet, which does not expose
# a comparable flag; its warnings are discussed in §\@ref(apx-b-convergence))
conv_tab <- aggregate(Converged ~ Model,
  data = final_results,
  FUN = function(x) 100 * mean(x, na.rm = TRUE)
)
```

```{r b2-best, include=FALSE}
# Model selection for B2. The choice weighs several criteria (see prose), so
# it is made explicitly rather than by argmin
# the stopifnot below verifies that the stated basis for the choice 
# (best winsorised RMSE at both caps and best deviance in the relative table)
# holds in the data, so a stale cache or changed configuration fails the knit
# rather than silently contradicting B2.
best_model <- "RR"
stopifnot(
  rel_tab$Model[which.min(rel_tab$RMSE_Win_95)] == best_model,
  rel_tab$Model[which.min(rel_tab$RMSE_Win_99)] == best_model,
  rel_tab$Model[which.min(rel_tab$Dev)] == best_model
)

# Headline numbers for the chosen model for inline reference
bm_rel <- rel_tab[rel_tab$Model == best_model, ]
bm_raw <- raw_tab[raw_tab$Model == best_model, ]
g2_raw <- raw_tab[raw_tab$Model == "GLM2", ]
bm_rmse95_gain <- 100 * (1 - bm_rel$RMSE_Win_95) # % improvement vs GLM2
bm_mae95_gain <- 100 * (1 - bm_rel$MAE_Win_95)

# Per-run win rate vs GLM2 on winsorised RMSE (95% cap)
bm_w95_winrate <- 100 * mean(
  rel_runs$RMSE_Win_95[rel_runs$Model == best_model] < 1,
  na.rm = TRUE
)

# Comparison values for the B2 prose
en_rel <- rel_tab[rel_tab$Model == "EN", ]
en_gap_w95 <- 100 * (en_rel$RMSE_Win_95 - bm_rel$RMSE_Win_95) # EN vs RR, pp
dsh_ae <- raw_tab$AE_Ratio[raw_tab$Model == "DSh"]
bm_ae_shortfall <- 100 * abs(bm_raw$AE_Ratio - 1) # % miscalibration
```

```{r b3-insample, include=FALSE}
# B3: in-sample analysis of the chosen model on all 1,000 observations ====
# The full dataset plays the role of the training set: splines, leave-one-out
# target encoding and standardisation are all built on the same 1,000 rows.
fe_full <- build_features(data_sev, test = NULL)
full_data <- fe_full$train
X_full <- model.matrix(glm_formula, full_data)
y_full <- full_data$Severity
w_full <- full_data$ClaimNb

# BE note: cv.glmnet randomises its internal folds so setting internal seed
# again here 
set.seed(group_num)
coefs_full <- fit_model_coefs(best_model, X_full, y_full, w_full)
coefs_full[is.na(coefs_full)] <- 0
mu_full <- exp(as.numeric(X_full %*% coefs_full))

# In-sample summary metrics
is_rmse <- sqrt(mean((y_full - mu_full)^2))
is_mae <- mean(abs(y_full - mu_full))
is_gini <- NormalisedWeightedGini(y_full, w_full, mu_full)
is_ae <- sum(y_full * w_full) / sum(mu_full * w_full)
is_dev <- sum(w_full * Gamma()$dev.resids(y_full, mu_full, 1))
null_mu <- weighted.mean(y_full, w_full)
is_dev_null <- sum(w_full * Gamma()$dev.resids(y_full, rep(null_mu, n_pol), 1))
is_pseudoR2 <- 1 - is_dev / is_dev_null

# Deviance residuals and calibration by predicted-severity decile
dev_res <- sign(y_full - mu_full) *
  sqrt(w_full * Gamma()$dev.resids(y_full, mu_full, 1))
decile_tab <- data.frame(y = y_full, mu = mu_full) |>
  dplyr::mutate(Decile = dplyr::ntile(mu, 10)) |>
  dplyr::group_by(Decile) |>
  dplyr::summarise(
    N = dplyr::n(),
    `Mean predicted` = mean(mu),
    `Mean actual` = mean(y),
    `AE ratio` = sum(y) / sum(mu),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# Values for the B3 prose
pred_lo <- min(mu_full)
pred_hi <- max(mu_full)
pred_span <- pred_hi - pred_lo
act_lo <- min(y_full)
act_hi <- max(y_full)
dec_pred_first <- decile_tab$`Mean predicted`[1]
dec_pred_last  <- decile_tab$`Mean predicted`[nrow(decile_tab)]
dec_act_min <- min(decile_tab$`Mean actual`)
dec_act_max <- max(decile_tab$`Mean actual`)
dec_ae_lo9  <- min(decile_tab$`AE ratio`[-nrow(decile_tab)])
dec_ae_hi9  <- max(decile_tab$`AE ratio`[-nrow(decile_tab)])
dec_ae_top  <- decile_tab$`AE ratio`[nrow(decile_tab)]

# GLM2 comparison fit for the figure (deterministic, no internal randomness)
mu_glm2 <- exp(as.numeric(
  X_full %*% fit_model_coefs("GLM2", X_full, y_full, w_full)
))
```

---

<!-- ## Executive summary {-} -->

<!-- TBI (going to do last) if required.  Thinking of this as being 1-2 paragraphs max. A bit like a scientific abstract to set the stage. -->

## Out-of-sample performance evaluation {#b-i}

### Data and preprocessing {#b-prep}

The French Motor Third-Party Liability (freMTPL2) frequency and severity
datasets were merged on policy ID after aggregating claim amounts per policy.
Policies with exactly one claim and a positive claim amount were retained, and a
random sample of `r fmt(n_pol, 0)` policies was drawn under our Group 
`r group_num` seed. This sample was fixed for all subsequent analysis, with the
cross-validation seeds inside the evaluation loop reshuffling only the fold
labels.

Three groups of features were then constructed. Continuous rating factors were
capped and log-transformed, with `VehPower` capped at 9, `BonusMalus` at 150
and `Density` floored at 1, to bound implausible values and squash the heavy
right tails typical of insurance covariates. Driver and vehicle age entered
through natural cubic splines with three degrees of freedom, allowing the
non-monotone age effects familiar from motor pricing. The high-cardinality
factors `Region`, `Area` and `VehBrand` were target-encoded with a
credibility-style smoother,
$$
\text{TE} = \lambda\,\bar y_{\text{group}} + (1-\lambda)\,\bar y_{\text{global}}
$$
with $\lambda = n/(n + \kappa)$ and $\kappa = 20$, computed leave-one-out on
training rows so that a policy never contributes to its own encoding. Target encoding also keeps
the design matrix compact. One-hot encoding these three factors would add
roughly thirty sparse columns to an 800-row training fold, worsening the
conditioning of the weighted information matrix that must be inverted at
every step of the iteratively reweighted least squares (IRLS) algorithm.

All continuous features were standardised using training-fold means and
standard deviations. Standardisation is not strictly required for any of the
fitted models, since fitted values depend on the data only through the linear
predictor, but it is retained for comparability with the released practice
code. Every preprocessing step was learned on training folds and frozen
before application to test folds, so no information leaks from test to train.
The robustness of these choices, covering the two variable caps, the spline
flexibility, the target-encoding smoother $\kappa$ and the standardisation
step, is examined in Appendix \@ref(apx-b-robustness). In **R** this was
implemented as follows:

```{r b-data-prep-show, ref.label="b-data-prep", echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
```

### Models and out-of-sample evaluation design {#b-design}

Nine models were fitted with a Gamma response and log link such that
$$
\hat y_i = \hat\mu_i = \exp(x_i^\top\hat\beta)
$$
The IRLS fit `GLM2` serves as the baseline. Ridge regression (RR) and elastic net (EN)
were fitted via `cv.glmnet`, with the penalty chosen by internal
cross-validation (`lambda.min`). The remaining six models are the shrinkage
GLMs of @Asimit2025GLMsolsviaShrinkage implemented in the `savvyGLM` package
(`St`, `DSh`, `SR`, `GSR`, `LW` and `QIS`). These estimators shrink the maximum-likelihood estimates towards structured targets in the spirit of the
mean and covariance shrinkage used in Coursework 1. Mechanically, these
estimators retain the IRLS structure of `glm.fit2` but replace the weighted
least squares (WLS) step at each iteration with a shrunk solution. `St` and
`DSh` rescale the WLS coefficients by scalars chosen to minimise mean squared
error, `SR` and `GSR` impose slab penalties along eigenvector directions, and
`LW` and `QIS` shrink the weighted information matrix before inversion. All
shrinkage intensities are available in closed form, so unlike RR and EN
no cross-validation is required. RR and EN are fitted by
coordinate descent, which is why their computation time (CT) and iteration
count (NoIt) are not directly comparable with those of the IRLS family.

Performance in each run is scored by the metrics specified in the brief. The
root mean squared error (RMSE) and mean absolute error (MAE) measure raw
prediction error, with winsorised variants capping actual and predicted
values at the 99th and 95th training-response percentiles (W99, W95) to limit
distortion from extreme claims. The Gamma deviance (Dev) measures
goodness-of-fit under the assumed distribution, the raw and normalised Gini
indices measure risk-ranking ability, and the actual-to-expected (AE) ratio
measures aggregate calibration, with a value of one indicating that total
predicted severity matches total observed severity.

Out-of-sample performance is estimated by 5-fold cross-validation replicated
100 times, giving `r fmt(n_runs, 0)` evaluation runs. In each replication the
`r fmt(n_pol, 0)` policies are partitioned into five folds, with 80% of
observations training the models and 20% testing them, and each fold serves
as the test set exactly once. Winsorisation caps for the robust error metrics are computed from the training response in each run. The evaluation loop is reproduced below, with results cached to an rds file and reloaded at knit time
under the final configuration (one-claim filter, group seed 
`r group_num`, $n = `r fmt(n_pol, 0)`$).

```{r b1-loop-show, ref.label="b1-loop", echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
```

Convergence warnings emitted by `glmnet` and by the `savvyGLM` step-halving
safeguard during the loop are expected behaviour of those fitters rather than
evidence of failure, and are discussed further in Appendix
\@ref(apx-b-convergence).

### Raw performance {#b1-raw}

Table \@ref(tab:b1-raw-table) reports each metric averaged over all
`r fmt(n_runs, 0)` runs. Because severity is heavy-tailed, the unwinsorised
RMSE is dominated by a handful of very large claims and separates the models
poorly, so the winsorised variants are the more informative measures of
accuracy. A second feature of the table shapes the whole comparison. Every
normalised Gini is negative, baseline included (the least negative is EN at
`r fmt(raw_tab$Gini_Norm[raw_tab$Model == "EN"], 3)`), whereas a model with
genuine ranking power has a normalised Gini in $(0, 1]$. At
$n = `r fmt(n_pol, 0)`$, policy-level claim severity therefore carries
essentially no rankable out-of-sample signal. This is an expected property of
individual severity, where most variation is claim-level randomness, rather
than a defect of any particular estimator. The Gini columns are retained for
completeness, and the comparison between models rests on the winsorised
errors, the Gamma deviance and the AE calibration ratio.

```{r b1-raw-table}
kb <- kable(raw_tab,
  row.names = FALSE, booktabs = TRUE,
  digits = c(NA, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 1, 4, 4, 3, 3, 1),
  caption = "Raw out-of-sample performance: mean of each metric over all 500 evaluation runs. The best value per column is shown in green (lower is better for the error, deviance, time and iteration metrics, higher for the Gini indices, and closest to one for the AE ratio). NoIt counts IRLS iterations for GLM2 and savvyGLM but coordinate-descent passes for glmnet, so it is not comparable across those two families.",
  col.names = c(
    "Model", "RMSE", "MAE", "RMSE W99", "RMSE W95", "MAE W99",
    "MAE W95", "Dev.", "Gini raw", "Gini norm", "AE", "CT (s)",
    "NoIt"
  )
) |>
  kable_styling(
    full_width = FALSE, position = "center", font_size = 8,
    latex_options = c("scale_down", "hold_position")
  ) |>
  pack_rows("Baseline", 1, 1) |>
  pack_rows("Penalised (glmnet)", 2, 3) |>
  pack_rows("Shrinkage GLMs (savvyGLM)", 4, 9)
for (j in seq_along(metric_cols)) {
  b <- best_flag(raw_tab[[metric_cols[j]]], metric_dir[metric_cols[j]])
  kb <- column_spec(kb, j + 1, bold = b, color = ifelse(b, "#1b9e77", "black"))
}
kb
```

```{r b1-rel-table}
kb <- kable(rel_tab_disp,
  row.names = FALSE, booktabs = TRUE, digits = 3,
  caption = "Relative out-of-sample performance: mean of per-run ratios versus GLM2 (GLM2 = 1 by construction). Values below one are better for the error, deviance, time and iteration metrics. The AE ratio is itself a ratio and the per-run GLM2 Gini is near zero and sign-varying, so ratios of these three columns are not meaningful. As they are judged from the raw table their cells are omitted here.",
  col.names = c(
    "Model", "RMSE", "MAE", "RMSE W99", "RMSE W95", "MAE W99",
    "MAE W95", "Dev.", "Gini raw", "Gini norm", "AE", "CT (s)", "NoIt"
  )
) |>
  kable_styling(
    full_width = FALSE, position = "center", font_size = 8,
    latex_options = c("scale_down", "hold_position")
  ) |>
  pack_rows("Baseline", 1, 1) |>
  pack_rows("Penalised (glmnet)", 2, 3) |>
  pack_rows("Shrinkage GLMs (savvyGLM)", 4, 9)
for (j in seq_along(metric_cols)) {
  if (metric_cols[j] %in% ratio_excluded) next
  b <- best_flag(rel_tab_disp[[metric_cols[j]]], metric_dir[metric_cols[j]])
  b[rel_tab_disp$Model == "GLM2"] <- FALSE
  kb <- column_spec(kb, j + 1, bold = b, color = ifelse(b, "#1b9e77", "black"))
}
kb
```


### Relative performance {#b1-rel}

Table \@ref(tab:b1-rel-table) shows the average of the
per-run ratios against the `GLM2` value in the same run for each metric, given by Eq. \@ref(eq:relative-performance).
\begin{equation}
\frac{1}{500}\sum_{j=1}^{500}
\frac{\text{Metric for Model}^{(j)}}{\text{Metric for GLM2}^{(j)}}
(\#eq:relative-performance)
\end{equation}
Because each ratio conditions on the same train/test split, fold-to-fold
noise cancels and the table isolates the systematic effect of the estimator.
Three columns are excluded from ratio form. The AE ratio is already a ratio,
so a further ratio has no calibration interpretation. The same exclusion
applies with greater force to the two Gini indices, whose per-run `GLM2`
denominator is small and frequently negative, so the resulting ratios flip
sign and explode. The mechanism is the same division by a near-zero quantity
that inflated the tangency leverage in Coursework 1 (Appendix
\@ref(apx-pathology)), with one important difference. In Coursework 1 the denominator was part of the portfolio construction, so the explosion was a genuine economic property of the strategy. Here it is an artefact of the
reporting convention and carries no information about the models. All three
metrics are therefore judged from the raw table (Table
\@ref(tab:b1-raw-table)) and their ratio cells are omitted.

Where the ratios are interpretable, a consistent ordering emerges. RR
delivers the largest systematic reductions in the winsorised RMSEs
(`r fmt(100 * (1 - rel_tab$RMSE_Win_95[rel_tab$Model == "RR"]), 1)`%
at the 95% cap) and in deviance, with EN a near-tie, while the `savvyGLM`
estimators cluster around parity with `GLM2` at a fraction of `glmnet`'s
computation time. This ordering is consistent with the evidence in
@Asimit2025GLMsolsviaShrinkage, which finds ridge regression dominant under
the log link used here in both simulation and the freMTPL2 application.

## Model comparison and selection {#b-b2}

With no model showing out-of-sample ranking power (Section \@ref(b1-raw)),
selection rests on tail-robust accuracy, deviance and calibration. On that basis
we select Ridge Regression (RR) as the "best" overall performer. RR posts the
best winsorised RMSE at both caps and the lowest deviance, beating `GLM2` on the
95%-capped RMSE in
`r fmt(bm_w95_winrate, 1)`% of the `r fmt(n_runs, 0)` runs with a
`r fmt(bm_rmse95_gain, 1)`% average reduction. Elastic net is practically
indistinguishable, within
`r fmt(en_gap_w95, 1)` percentage points on the 95%-capped RMSE with marginally
better calibration, and would be an equally defensible choice. RR's aggregate
calibration (AE `r fmt(bm_raw$AE_Ratio, 3)`) trails DSh
(`r fmt(dsh_ae, 3)`), but a uniform `r fmt(bm_ae_shortfall, 0)`% shortfall is
corrected by rebasing the premium level, whereas DSh cannot recover RR's
accuracy. That penalised L2 beats the bespoke shrinkage GLMs is itself
informative. At n = `r fmt(n_pol, 0)`, with a correlated spline and
target-encoded design and almost no severity signal, the optimal shrinkage is
heavy. Ridge tunes its intensity directly against predictive loss by
cross-validation, while the `savvyGLM` plug-in intensities shrink towards
maximum-likelihood targets that inherit the noise, the regime in which
@Asimit2025GLMsolsviaShrinkage likewise find ridge dominant under the log
link. RR's computation time, under a second per fit, is immaterial.

## In-sample performance analysis {#b-b3}

The chosen model, `r best_model`, was refitted to the full sample of
`r fmt(n_pol, 0)` policies. The preprocessing described in Section
\@ref(b-prep) was applied unchanged, with the splines, leave-one-out target
encoding and standardisation now learned from all `r fmt(n_pol, 0)` rows and
the penalty re-selected by `cv.glmnet` under our group seed (`r group_num`) so
that the fit was stable between knits. Its fitted severities $\hat y_i$ are
compared with the observed claims $y_i$ below.

```{r b3-insample-summary, include=FALSE}
# BE note: note that this is dependent upon is_dev_null
# It's also a duplication or b3-insample.  
# Need to update in two places currently - look to fix in future
# 
# In-sample metrics for the selected model and the unpenalised baseline,
# computed on the same full-sample fit used in the figure below.
is_metrics <- function(mu) {
  dev <- sum(w_full * Gamma()$dev.resids(y_full, mu, 1))
  c(
    RMSE = sqrt(mean((y_full - mu)^2)),
    MAE = mean(abs(y_full - mu)),
    AE = sum(y_full * w_full) / sum(mu * w_full),
    Gini = NormalisedWeightedGini(y_full, w_full, mu),
    PseudoR2 = 1 - dev / is_dev_null
  )
}
m_rr <- is_metrics(mu_full)
m_g2 <- is_metrics(mu_glm2)

is_summary <- data.frame(
  Metric = c(
    "RMSE (EUR)", "MAE (EUR)", "AE ratio", "Normalised Gini",
    "Deviance reduction vs intercept-only"
  ),
  RR = c(
    fmt(m_rr["RMSE"], 0), fmt(m_rr["MAE"], 0), fmt(m_rr["AE"], 3),
    fmt(m_rr["Gini"], 3), paste0(fmt(100 * m_rr["PseudoR2"], 1), "%")
  ),
  GLM2 = c(
    fmt(m_g2["RMSE"], 0), fmt(m_g2["MAE"], 0), fmt(m_g2["AE"], 3),
    fmt(m_g2["Gini"], 3), paste0(fmt(100 * m_g2["PseudoR2"], 1), "%")
  ),
  check.names = FALSE
)
```

At the portfolio level the model is almost perfectly calibrated. The
actual-to-expected ratio is `r fmt(is_ae, 3)`, so total predicted severity
matches total observed severity to within `r fmt(100 * abs(is_ae - 1), 1)`%.
Beneath that aggregate, however, the model differentiates very little. The
Gamma deviance falls only `r fmt(100 * is_pseudoR2, 1)`% below the
intercept-only model, and the `r fmt(n_pol, 0)` fitted severities span a band
of roughly `r fmt(pred_span, 0)` euros (€`r fmt(pred_lo, 0)` to
€`r fmt(pred_hi, 0)`) around the portfolio mean, against actual claims ranging
from €`r fmt(act_lo, 0)` to €`r fmt(act_hi, 0)`. Fitted to all
`r fmt(n_pol, 0)` rows, `cv.glmnet` selects a heavy penalty and shrinks the
model nearly to the intercept. This is the in-sample face of the selection
argument in \@ref(b-b2). With almost no rankable severity signal, the
loss-minimising fit sits close to a constant. `r best_model` won the
out-of-sample comparison precisely because it declines to fit noise. Absolute
errors remain large (RMSE `r fmt(is_rmse, 0)`, MAE `r fmt(is_mae, 0)`), an
unavoidable feature of individual-level severity, where most of the variance is
claim-level randomness that is not predictable from any policyholder
characteristic present in the data.

Table \@ref(tab:b3-summary-table) places the in-sample metrics beside the
unpenalised `GLM2` baseline fitted to the same rows. `GLM2` posts the lower
in-sample RMSE, the larger deviance reduction and the higher normalised Gini,
yet Table \@ref(tab:b1-raw-table) showed it losing to `r best_model` on all the
error metrics. The additional in-sample fit that `GLM2` achieves does not
generalise, which is the clearest demonstration of what the ridge penalty
removes. `GLM2` also miscalibrates in aggregate (AE
`r fmt(m_g2["AE"], 3)`), over-predicting total severity by 
`r fmt(100 * abs(m_g2["AE"] - 1), 1)`%, so the penalty improves the portfolio 
level as well as the policy level.

```{r b3-summary-table}
kable(is_summary,
  booktabs = TRUE, 
  row.names = FALSE, 
  align = c("l", "r", "r"),
  col.names = c("Metric", best_model, "GLM2"),
  caption = paste0(
    "In-sample performance of the selected ", best_model,
    " model and the unpenalised GLM2 baseline, both fitted to all ",
    fmt(n_pol, 0), " policies. GLM2 fits the sample more closely on every ",
    "measure, but that additional fit does not survive out of sample."
  )
) |>
  kable_styling(
    full_width = FALSE, position = "center",
    latex_options = "hold_position"
  )
```

```{r b3-fig-panels, fig.cap = paste0("In-sample diagnostics for ", best_model, " on the full sample. Panel A: fitted against actual severity (log scales) for the unpenalised GLM2 baseline and the selected ", best_model, " model, with the dashed line marking perfect prediction. Panel B: ", best_model," deviance residuals against predicted severity."), fig.width=6, fig.height=5.5}
# ( ˶ˆᗜˆ˵ )
pa_df <- rbind(
  data.frame(Model = "GLM2 (baseline)", mu = mu_glm2, y = y_full),
  data.frame(Model = "RR (selected)", mu = mu_full, y = y_full)
)
p_pa <- ggplot(pa_df, aes(mu, y)) +
  geom_point(alpha = .3, size = .8, colour = "#0072B2") +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  scale_x_log10(labels = scales::comma) +
  scale_y_log10(labels = scales::comma) +
  facet_wrap(~Model) +
  labs(x = "Predicted severity (EUR, log scale)", y = "Actual severity (EUR, log scale)") +
  theme_minimal()
p_res <- ggplot(data.frame(mu = mu_full, r = dev_res), aes(mu, r)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linewidth = .3) +
  geom_point(alpha = .3, size = .8, colour = "#0072B2") +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::comma) +
  labs(x = "Predicted severity, RR (EUR)", y = "Deviance residual") +
  theme_minimal()
p_pa / p_res + plot_layout(heights = c(1.2, 1)) + plot_annotation(tag_levels = "A")
```

Figure \@ref(fig:b3-fig-panels) illustrates these points visually by placing
`r best_model` beside the unpenalised `GLM2` fitted to the same data. `GLM2`
spreads its predictions across an order of magnitude while `r best_model`
compresses them to a narrow vertical band. However, Table
\@ref(tab:b1-raw-table) shows that the wider spread carries no out-of-sample
ranking power. It is fitted noise, and removing it is exactly what the penalty
is for. Panel B plots the deviance residuals. With near-constant predictions
these display the dispersion of individual claims around a single level, rather
than acting as a diagnostic of the link specification. The cloud is centred on
zero with the positive skew of the severity tail.

```{r b3-decile-table}
kable(decile_tab,
  booktabs = TRUE, digits = c(0, 0, 0, 0, 3),
  align = c("c", "c", "c", "c", "c"),
  col.names = c(
    "Decile", "N", "Mean predicted (EUR)",
    "Mean actual (EUR)", "AE ratio"
  ),
  caption = paste0(
    "In-sample calibration of ", best_model,
    " by decile of predicted severity, with the decile actual-to-expected ratio."
  )
) |>
  kable_styling(
    full_width = FALSE, position = "center",
    latex_options = "hold_position"
  )
```

Table \@ref(tab:b3-decile-table) sorts policies into deciles of predicted
severity. Mean predicted severity is essentially flat, rising from
€`r fmt(dec_pred_first, 0)` in the bottom decile to €`r fmt(dec_pred_last, 0)` 
in the top, while mean actual severity swings from €`r fmt(dec_act_min, 0)` to
€`r fmt(dec_act_max, 0)`. The decile AE ratios are patternless across the lower
nine deciles (`r fmt(dec_ae_lo9, 2)` to `r fmt(dec_ae_hi9, 2)`) before jumping
to `r fmt(dec_ae_top, 2)` in the top decile. With a near-constant predictor,
decile membership mostly slices noise, and the top decile happens to capture a
cluster of large claims. That cluster is the sole source of the modest
in-sample normalised Gini of `r fmt(is_gini, 3)`, a figure that fails to
survive out of sample, where every model's Gini is negative.

At this volume, average claim severity is best rated near-flat. In the standard
frequency-severity decomposition, risk differentiation is carried by the
frequency model, and exposure to the largest claims is managed through
large-claim loadings or layered reinsurance rather than the policy-level
severity model. As such, `r best_model`'s behaviour is not a defect but the
coherent endpoint of the analysis. It is an almost perfectly calibrated
aggregate level, and an honest refusal to manufacture differentiation the data
cannot support.


<!-- ```{=html} -->
<!-- # References -->
<!-- <div id="refs"></div> -->
<!-- ``` -->

<!-- ```{=latex} -->
<!-- \bibliography{references.bib} -->
<!-- ``` -->

```{=latex}
\clearpage
\newpage
\section*{Appendices}
\addcontentsline{toc}{section}{Appendices}
```

# (APPENDIX) Appendices {-}

```{=latex}
\titleformat{\section}[block]{\LARGE\bfseries}{\thesection}{1em}{}
\renewcommand{\thesection}{\arabic{section}}
\renewcommand{\thesubsection}{\thesection.\arabic{subsection}}
\renewcommand{\thesubsubsection}{\thesubsection.\arabic{subsubsection}}
\setcounter{section}{0}
```

# Coursework 1: Supplemental methodology {#apd-supplementary-methods}

## Mean-vector estimators {#apx-method-mean-vec-estimators}

Every estimator starts from the sample mean vector, $\bar X$. The shrinkage
estimators adjust $\bar X$ using the unbiased sample covariance of the returns,
$S$, and the grand mean $\bar X_g = \tfrac{1}{p}\mathbf 1^\top \bar X$, the
average of $\bar X$ across the $p$ assets. Each estimator maps the $n \times p$
matrix of training returns to its own length-$p$ estimate, and the estimators
are compared by mean squared error (MSE).

```{asis, echo=knitr::is_html_output()}
| Estimator                                                   | Shrinks toward                | How it shrinks                                             | Loss      | Source          |
| ----------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------- | --------- | --------------- |
| Sample mean                                                 | no shrinkage                  | Unbiased. High variance when $p \approx n$                 | -         | -               |
| Stein Multiplicative Shrinkage `St-MSh`                     | zero                          | One scalar $\hat c \in [0,1)$ applied to every asset       | MSE       | @Asimit15062026 |
| Diagonal Multiplicative Shrinkage `D-MSh`                   | zero                          | Asset-specific scalars $\hat c_k$ (suits differing scales) | MSE       | @Asimit15062026 |
| One-Parameter Linear Shrinkage `O-LSh`                      | grand mean                    | One intensity $\hat\delta$                                 | MSE       | @Asimit15062026 |
| Two-Parameter Linear Shrinkage `T-LSh`                      | grand mean, rescaled          | Intensity $\hat\delta$ plus target scaling $\hat\xi$       | MSE       | @Asimit15062026 |
| The shrinkage estimator in @Wang2014 `Wang`                 | grand mean                    | One intensity, estimated by U-statistics                   | MSE       | @Wang2014       |
| The shrinkage estimator in @Bodnar2019 `BOP`                | precision-weighted grand mean | One intensity, built from $S^{+}$                          | invariant | @Bodnar2019     |
