| The Bayes-Stein shrinkage estimator in @Jorion1986 `Jorion` | precision-weighted grand mean | Bayes-Stein intensity                                      | invariant | @Jorion1986     |
```

```{r apx-mean-estimator-table, eval=knitr::is_latex_output()}
est_tab <- data.frame(
  Estimator = c(
    "Sample mean",
    "Stein Multiplicative Shrinkage (\\greentt{St-MSh})",
    "Diagonal Multiplicative Shrinkage (\\greentt{D-MSh})",
    "One-Parameter Linear Shrinkage (\\greentt{O-LSh})",
    "Two-Parameter Linear Shrinkage (\\greentt{T-LSh})",
    "Shrinkage estimator of Wang et al. (\\greentt{Wang})",
    "Shrinkage estimator of Bodnar et al. (\\greentt{BOP})",
    "Bayes--Stein estimator (\\greentt{Jorion})"
  ),
  Target = c(
    "no shrinkage",
    "zero",
    "zero",
    "grand mean",
    "grand mean, rescaled",
    "grand mean",
    "precision-weighted grand mean",
    "precision-weighted grand mean"
  ),
  How = c(
    "Unbiased; high variance when $p \\approx n$",
    "One scalar $\\hat c \\in [0,1)$ applied to every asset",
    "Asset-specific scalars $\\hat c_k$ (suits differing scales)",
    "One intensity $\\hat\\delta$",
    "Intensity $\\hat\\delta$ plus target scaling $\\hat\\xi$",
    "One intensity, estimated by U-statistics",
    "One intensity, built from $S^{+}$",
    "Bayes--Stein intensity"
  ),
  Loss = c(
    "---", "MSE", "MSE", "MSE", "MSE", "MSE",
    "invariant", "invariant"
  ),
  # manually coded
  Source = c( 
    "---",
    "\\citet{Asimit15062026}", "\\citet{Asimit15062026}",
    "\\citet{Asimit15062026}", "\\citet{Asimit15062026}",
    "\\citet{Wang2014}", "\\citet{Bodnar2019}", "\\citet{Jorion1986}"
  )
)
kable(est_tab,
  booktabs = TRUE, escape = FALSE, row.names = FALSE,
  caption = "Mean-vector estimators: shrinkage target, mechanism, and the loss each is designed to minimise.",
  col.names = c("Estimator", "Shrinks toward", "How it shrinks", "Loss", "Source"),
  linesep = ""
) |>
  kable_styling(
    full_width = FALSE, position = "center",
    font_size = 9, latex_options = "hold_position"
  ) |>
  column_spec(1, width = "13em") |>
  column_spec(2, width = "8em") |>
  column_spec(3, width = "13em")
```


The four estimators of @Asimit15062026 take the following explicit forms:

$$
\hat\mu^{\text{St-MSh}} = \hat c\,\bar X,\qquad
\hat c = \frac{\|\bar X\|_p^2}{\|\bar X\|_p^2 + \tfrac{1}{np}\operatorname{tr}(S)},
\qquad \|\bar X\|_p^2 = \tfrac{1}{p}\bar X^\top \bar X,
$$
$$
\hat\mu^{\text{D-MSh}}_k = \hat c_k\,\bar X_k,\qquad
\hat c_k = \frac{\bar X_k^2}{\bar X_k^2 + \tfrac{1}{n}S_{kk}},
$$
$$
\hat\mu^{\text{O-LSh}} = (1-\hat\delta)\bar X + \hat\delta\,\bar X_g\mathbf 1,
\qquad
\hat\mu^{\text{T-LSh}} = (1-\hat\delta)\bar X + \hat\delta\hat\xi\,\bar X_g\mathbf 1.
$$

The amount of shrinkage in each estimator is set by a shrinkage intensity, 
$\hat c$ or $\hat c_k$. This scalar multiplies the sample mean in `St-MSh` and
`D-MSh`, or the weight $\hat\delta$ that mixes the sample mean with the target
in `O-LSh` and `T-LSh`. All four intensities follow the same signal-to-noise
trade-off. They shrink harder when the sample mean is noisier and less when the
signal is stronger. `T-LSh` extends `O-LSh` by also scaling the target, and its
oracle MSE (the MSE it would attain if the true mean and covariance were known)
is strictly lower than that of `O-LSh`.

The other three estimators, `Wang`, `BOP` and `Jorion`, are established
benchmarks. `Wang` shares the target of `O-LSh`, the grand mean, but estimates
its intensity with U-statistics rather than the sample plug-in estimates used
above [@Wang2014]. `BOP` and `Jorion` instead shrink toward a precision-weighted
grand mean, a minimum-variance combination formed with $S^{+}$ for `BOP` and
$\tilde S^{-1}$ for `Jorion` [@Bodnar2019; @Jorion1986]. Both optimise the
invariant loss rather than the MSE, weighting estimation errors by the precision
matrix, the inverse covariance.

## Why `St-MSh` reproduces the sample-mean Sharpe ratios exactly {#apx-stmsh-vs-sample}

The identical Sample and `St-MSh` rows in Table \@ref(tab:a-headline-sharpe) are
not a coincidence, nor a bug. The same identity appears in Table 3 of
@Asimit15062026 where the `St-MSh` and Sample rows are identical in both the
mean-variance and tangency panels (e.g. 0.274/1.352 in Panel A and 0.385/1.325
in Panel B), despite their different universe (441 stocks), frequency (daily)
and window scheme.

This occurs because `St-MSh` multiplies the sample mean by a single scalar, 
$\hat\mu^{\text{St-MSh}} = \hat c\,\bar X$, and this scalar is strictly
positive, since the numerator of $\hat c$ is a squared norm and the denominator
adds a sum of sample variances. Because the portfolio map is linear in
$\hat\mu$, the scalar passes straight through to the weights,
$$
w_{MV}^{\text{St-MSh}}
  = \Sigma^{-1}(\hat c\,\bar X)
  = \hat c\, w_{MV}^{\text{Sample}},
$$
and a portfolio's weekly returns scale with its weights,
$r_t(\hat c\,w) = \hat c\, r_t(w)$. The Sharpe ratio is a ratio of a mean to a
standard deviation, so any positive constant cancels:
$$
\mathrm{SR}(\hat c\,w)
  = \frac{\hat c\,\bar r}{\hat c\, s_r}\sqrt{52}
  = \mathrm{SR}(w).
$$
The two strategies therefore have the same Sharpe ratio in every window, hence
the same mean and the same standard deviation across windows. 
<!-- Note to Vali: this is verified numerically: `r stm_check` -->
For the tangency portfolio the cancellation happens one step earlier, in the
weights themselves,
$$
w_{TP}^{\text{St-MSh}}
  = \frac{\hat c\,\Sigma^{-1}\bar X}{\hat c\,\mathbf 1^\top\Sigma^{-1}\bar X}
  = w_{TP}^{\text{Sample}},
$$
As a result `TP_StMSh` and `TP_Sample` are the same portfolio with identical leverage, turnover and net-short windows, as seen in Tables \@ref(tab:cond-table) and
\@ref(tab:scale-metrics). Since $\hat c > 0$, `St-MSh` also flips sign in exactly the same windows as the sample mean.

An interesting observation is that because the scalar $\hat c$ is
re-estimated each window the stitched `MV_StMSh` series is not a global
rescaling of `MV_Sample` as each month is scaled by a different constant. The
per-window Sharpe statistics are identical, but the stitched Sharpe in Table
\@ref(tab:scale-metrics) differs slightly, and every scale-sensitive metric
(leverage, volatility, certainty-equivalent) shrinks by roughly $\hat c$ for the
mean-variance portfolio. The other estimators avoid this equivalence
because they change the direction. `D-MSh` rescales each asset differently, and
the linear-shrinkage estimators mix $\bar X$ with a nonzero target. `St-MSh`'s
shrinkage is visible in the scale-sensitive mean-variance metrics, but it is
invisible to any scale-invariant evaluation of a linear portfolio rule.

## Covariance conditioning: `cov1Para` vs sample covariance {#apx-cond}

With the mean estimators and portfolio construction held fixed, replacing
`cov1Para` by the sample covariance sharply worsens conditioning and
implementability. Averaged across windows, the condition number of the
covariance estimate is
`r fmt(cond_mean_shrink, 1)` under `cov1Para` against
`r fmt(cond_mean_sample, 1)` under the sample covariance, with medians of
`r fmt(cond_med_shrink, 1)` and `r fmt(cond_med_sample, 1)`. Table
\@ref(tab:cond-table) shows, for each mean estimator, the average gross
leverage and turnover of the tangency portfolio under each covariance estimator,
along with its Sharpe ratio on the stitched out-of-sample series.

```{r cond-table}
kbl_cond <- cond_tab[, c(
  "GrossLev_cov1Para", "Turnover_cov1Para", "OOS_Sharpe_cov1Para",
  "GrossLev_sample", "Turnover_sample", "OOS_Sharpe_sample"
)]
kable(kbl_cond,
  digits = 2, booktabs = TRUE,
  caption = "Tangency-portfolio implementability by covariance estimator. Gross leverage and turnover are averaged across windows, and Sharpe is computed on the stitched out-of-sample series.",
  col.names = c("Gross lev.", "Turnover", "Sharpe", "Gross lev.", "Turnover", "Sharpe")
) |>
  kable_styling(full_width = FALSE, position = "center", latex_options = "hold_position") |>
  add_header_above(c(" " = 1, "cov1Para" = 3, "Sample covariance" = 3))
```

```{=latex}
% \newpage
```

## Scale-sensitive out-of-sample metrics {#apx-scale}

The annualised Sharpe ratio is invariant to positive rescaling of the weight
vector, so it cannot distinguish portfolios that differ sharply in leverage,
trading and exposure. However these are the features that determine whether a strategy can be run. Table \@ref(tab:scale-metrics) shows metrics that respond
to scale computed on the stitched out-of-sample series, with the `r n_windows`
test windows concatenated in time order. As such, the Sharpe ratios differ
from the per-window average shown in Table \@ref(tab:a-headline-sharpe).

The certainty-equivalent (CE) return is the risk-adjusted return an investor
with risk aversion $\gamma$ would trade the strategy for, computed as the
annualised excess return minus $\tfrac{\gamma}{2}$ times the annualised
variance. This uses the same $\gamma = 1$ as the portfolio construction
(Section \@ref(a3)). Gross leverage (the sum of absolute weights) and
maximum absolute weight are averaged across windows. Turnover is the change in
weights per monthly rebalance. The net-short count is the number of windows in
which the tangency normaliser $c = \mathbf 1^\top\Sigma^{-1}\hat\mu$ is
negative.

Turnover compares target weights at consecutive rebalances and does not account
for the drift of holdings by intervening returns. This approximates true
trading volume and because the same simplification applies to every strategy,
cross-strategy comparisons are unaffected.


```{r scale-metrics}
# options(knitr.kable.NA = "---")
# best_sr <- tab_full$Sharpe == max(tab_full$Sharpe, na.rm = TRUE)
# best_ce <- tab_full$CE == max(tab_full$CE, na.rm = TRUE)
# kable(tab_full,
#   digits = c(2, 2, 2, 1, 1, 2, 1, 0),
#   format.args = list(big.mark = ","), booktabs = TRUE,
#   caption = "Scale-sensitive out-of-sample metrics, full period (annualised weekly excess returns, omega = 1). Sharpe is on the stitched OOS series; CE is the realised certainty-equivalent; leverage and max weight are window averages; turnover is per monthly rebalance; net-short counts windows with negative sum(z).",
#   col.names = c(
#     "Ann. ex. ret.", "Ann. vol.", "Sharpe", "CE",
#     "Gross lev.", "Max wt", "Turnover", "Net-short"
#   ),
#   linesep = c("\\addlinespace", rep(c("", "\\addlinespace"), length(mean_estimators)))
# ) |>
#   kable_styling(
#     full_width = FALSE, position = "center", font_size = 9,
#     latex_options = c("scale_down", "hold_position")
#   ) |>
#   add_header_above(c(" " = 1, "Performance" = 4, "Implementability" = 4)) |>
#   column_spec(3, bold = best_sr, color = ifelse(best_sr, "#1b9e77", "black")) |>
#   column_spec(4, bold = best_ce, color = ifelse(best_ce, "#1b9e77", "black"))

tf <- data.frame(
  Strategy = sub("^(MV|TP)_", "", rownames(tab_full)),
  tab_full,
  row.names = NULL,
  check.names = FALSE
)

rank_best <- function(x, highest_is_best = TRUE) {
  u <- sort(unique(x[!is.na(x)]), decreasing = highest_is_best)
  match(x, u)
}
# Green -> Orange -> Black
color_top2 <- function(x, highest_is_best = TRUE) {
  r <- rank_best(x, highest_is_best)
  col <- rep("black", length(x))
  col[!is.na(r) & r == 1L] <- "#1b9e77"
  col[!is.na(r) & r == 2L] <- "#d95f02"
  col
}
bold_top2 <- function(x, highest_is_best = TRUE) {
  r <- rank_best(x, highest_is_best)
  !is.na(r) & r <= 2L
}

style_map <- list(
  list(col = 2, hi = TRUE), # Ann. excess return
  list(col = 3, hi = FALSE), # Ann. volatility
  list(col = 4, hi = TRUE), # Sharpe
  list(col = 5, hi = TRUE), # Certainty equivalent
  list(col = 6, hi = FALSE), # Gross leverage
  list(col = 7, hi = FALSE), # Max weight
  list(col = 8, hi = FALSE) # Turnover
  # list(col = 9, hi = FALSE) # Net-short count
)

# best_sr <- tf$Sharpe == max(tf$Sharpe, na.rm = TRUE)
# best_ce <- tf$CE     == max(tf$CE,     na.rm = TRUE)

kt <- kable(tf,
  digits = c(0, 3, 3, 3, 1, 1, 2, 1, 0), # leading 0 for Strategy
  format.args = list(big.mark = ","), booktabs = TRUE, row.names = FALSE,
  caption = "Scale-sensitive out-of-sample metrics over the full period, using annualised weekly excess returns with risk aversion gamma = 1. The Sharpe ratio is computed on the stitched out-of-sample series. The certainty-equivalent return is the annualised excess return minus half the annualised variance. Gross leverage (sum of absolute weights) and maximum absolute weight are averaged across windows. Turnover is measured per monthly rebalance. The net-short count is the number of windows with a negative tangency normaliser. Within each column, the best and second-best values are shown in green and orange respectively.",
  col.names = c(
    "Strategy", "Ann. ex. ret.", "Ann. vol.", "Sharpe", "Cert. equiv.",
    "Gross lev.", "Max wt", "Turnover", "Net-short"
  ),
  linesep = ""
) |>
  kable_styling(
    full_width = FALSE, position = "center", font_size = 9,
    latex_options = c("scale_down", "hold_position")
  ) |>
  add_header_above(c(" " = 1, "Performance" = 4, "Implementability" = 4)) |>
  pack_rows(index = c(
    "Benchmark" = 1,
    "Mean-variance portfolio" = length(est_order),
    "Tangency portfolio" = length(est_order)
  ))
  # column_spec(4, bold = best_sr, color = ifelse(best_sr, "#1b9e77", "black")) |>
  # column_spec(5, bold = best_ce, color = ifelse(best_ce, "#1b9e77", "black"))

for (s in style_map) {
  x  <- tf[[s$col]]
  kt <- column_spec(kt, s$col, bold = bold_top2(x, s$hi), color = color_top2(x, s$hi))
}
kt
```

## Tangency sign flips {#apx-inout}

The tangency weights are $\Sigma^{-1}\hat\mu / c$ with 
$c = \mathbf 1^\top\Sigma^{-1}\hat\mu$. When $c$ is negative the construction
returns the minimum-Sharpe portfolio rather than the maximum-Sharpe one, so the
in-sample Sharpe ratio becomes large and negative. Counting these net-short
windows is a useful diagnostic, because they tend to cluster in high-volatility
regimes, exactly where the equal-weighted portfolio does well by never
optimising. Table \@ref(tab:inout-table) shows the share of windows that flip
and the average out-of-sample Sharpe in flipped versus non-flipped windows, for
each estimator. 

Figure \@ref(fig:inout-fig) plots in-sample against out-of-sample Sharpe, with
net-short windows highlighted. Out-of-sample Sharpe sits in a narrow band around
zero across the full in-sample range, with no upward trend, showing that a
strong in-sample Sharpe does not translate into a strong out-of-sample one. The flip windows separate the estimators by design. For the
mean-shrinkage estimators (`St-MSh`, `D-MSh`, `O-LSh`, `T-LSh`) and `Jorion`,
the flips concentrate at negative in-sample Sharpe, so these methods turn
net-short mainly when recent performance was poor. `Wang` and `BOP` behave in
the opposite way, flipping at positive in-sample Sharpe, and `BOP` flips far
more often than any other estimator 
(`r fmt(inout_summary$"Windows flipped (%)"[inout_summary$estimator == "BOP"],1)`% 
of windows against 
`r fmt(inout_summary$"Windows flipped (%)"[inout_summary$estimator == "Wang"],1)`%
for `Wang`). This split aligns with the loss functions for each estimator
(Appendix \@ref(apx-method-mean-vec-estimators)). Estimators that shrink toward
the grand mean under mean-squared error flip in one regime, and the two that
shrink toward the unit vector under the invariant loss flip in the other.

As before the Sample and `St-MSh` rows coincide exactly. As $\hat c > 0$, the sign
of $c = \mathbf 1^\top\Sigma^{-1}\hat\mu$ is unchanged by the shrinkage, so the
two estimators flip in the same windows (Appendix
\@ref(apx-stmsh-vs-sample)).

```{r inout-table}
kable(inout_summary,
  digits = 2, booktabs = TRUE, 
  caption = "Tangency sign-flip diagnostic by estimator: share of net-short windows and average out-of-sample Sharpe in flipped versus non-flipped windows.",
  col.names = c(
    "Estimator", "Windows flipped (\\%)",
    "OOS Sharpe (flipped)", "OOS Sharpe (not flipped)"
  ),
  escape = FALSE
) |>
  kable_styling(full_width = FALSE, position = "center", latex_options = "hold_position")
```

```{r inout-fig, fig.cap="In-sample versus out-of-sample annualised Sharpe (tangency portfolios), by estimator. Net-short windows (sign flips) are highlighted.", fig.width=6.5, fig.height=4.5}
ggplot(diag_df, aes(IS, OOS, color = flip)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linewidth = 0.3) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linewidth = 0.3) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, size = 1) +
  facet_wrap(~estimator) +
  labs(x = "In-sample Sharpe", y = "Out-of-sample Sharpe", color = "Sign flip") +
  theme_minimal()
```

## The near-zero normaliser {#apx-pathology}

Distinct from the sign flip, the tangency weights explode when $c$ passes close
to zero rather than merely reversing. Tangency gross leverage in window $k$ is
$\sum_i |z_i| / |c_k|$, so as $c_k \to 0$ the leverage diverges and every
moment-based metric (return, volatility, certainty-equivalent, leverage,
turnover) is dominated by that one window. The closest approach to zero across
windows is smallest for `D-MSh`. 
At window `r k_bad` (`r format(date_bad, "%d %B %Y")`),
$c \approx `r formatC(sumz_bad, format = "f", digits = 4)`$ drives `TP_DMSh`
gross leverage to `r fmt(lev_bad, 0)`. Table
\@ref(tab:pathology-table) lists the minimum $|c|$ per estimator.

```{r pathology-table}
patho <- data.frame(
  Estimator = est_order,
  `Min |c|` = round(as.numeric(min_sumz[est_order]), 4),
  check.names = FALSE
)
kable(patho,
  row.names = FALSE, booktabs = TRUE,
  caption = "Closest approach of $c = \\mathbf{1}^\\top \\Sigma^{-1}\\hat\\mu$ to zero, by estimator (smaller means a more unstable tangency window)."
) |>
  kable_styling(full_width = FALSE, position = "center", latex_options = "hold_position")
```

## Sample composition and the full universe {#apx-stocks}

Figure \@ref(fig:apx-a-stock-plots) shows how our sample compares to the wider context of the full estimation universe. The top panel compares the equal-weighted cumulative
return of the `r no_companies` Group `r group_num` stocks against the full
`r ncol(returns_all)`-stock universe, both re-based to 1 and shown on a
log axis. The
full-universe series is an equal-weighted proxy for the market rather than the
cap-weighted S&P 500. The bottom panel decomposes the full universe into its
GICS sectors on the same basis. The shaded bands mark the financial-crisis and
pandemic sub-periods. The sector paths show why our sample's light Technology
weighting muted its pandemic Sharpe ratios. The technology-led sectors that
compounded fastest after 2020 are exactly the ones our draw underweights
(Figure \@ref(fig:gics-fig)). 

The full list of our `r no_companies` companies, grouped by GICS sector, is
given in Table \@ref(tab:stocks-table).

```{=latex}
\newpage
```

```{r apx-a-stock-plots, fig.cap = paste0("Rebased equal-weighted cumulative returns, ",format(min(dates), "%B %Y"), " to ", format(max(dates), "%B %Y"),", on a log axis. Top: the ", no_companies, " Group ", group_num," stocks against the full ", ncol(returns_all),"-stock universe. Bottom: the full universe split by GICS sector. ", "Shaded bands mark the financial-crisis and pandemic sub-periods."), fig.width=6.5, fig.height=7.5}
regimes <- data.frame(
  Regime = c("Financial crisis", "Pandemic"),
  xmin = c(fc_lo, pd_lo),
  xmax = c(fc_hi, pd_hi)
)

cum_df <- data.frame(
  Date = dates,
  `Group 12 (100)` = cumprod(1 + rowMeans(returns100)),
  `Full universe (441)` = cumprod(1 + rowMeans(returns_all)),
  check.names = FALSE
)

long_cum <- pivot_longer(
  cum_df, -Date,
  names_to = "Universe", values_to = "Growth"
)
ylims <- range(long_cum$Growth, na.rm = TRUE)

p1 <- ggplot(
  pivot_longer(cum_df, -Date, names_to = "Universe", values_to = "Growth"),
  aes(Date, Growth, color = Universe)
) +
  geom_rect(
    data = regimes,
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    # manually tweaked
    aes(
      xmin = xmin, xmax = xmax, ymin = ylims[1], ymax = ylims[2], fill = Regime
    ),
    alpha = 0.08
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    breaks = c("Financial crisis", "Pandemic"),
    values = c("Financial crisis" = "red", "Pandemic" = "blue")
  ) +
  geom_line() +
  scale_y_log10() +
  labs(y = "Rebased values (log $)", x = NULL) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "top", legend.title = element_blank()) +
  guides(color = guide_legend(order = 1), fill = guide_legend(order = 2))

sec_map <- setNames(gics[["GICS Sector Name"]], gics$DA441_column_name)
sectors <- sec_map[colnames(returns_all)]
stopifnot(!anyNA(sectors))

sect_cum <- sapply(sort(unique(sectors)), function(s) {
  cumprod(1 + rowMeans(returns_all[, sectors == s, drop = FALSE]))
})

sect_df <- data.frame(Date = dates, sect_cum, check.names = FALSE)

p2 <- ggplot(
  pivot_longer(sect_df, -Date, names_to = "Sector", values_to = "Growth"),
  aes(Date, Growth, color = Sector)
) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.5) +
  scale_y_log10() +
  labs(y = "Rebased values (log $)", x = NULL) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom", legend.title = element_blank()) +
  guides(color = guide_legend(nrow = 3))

p1 / p2
```

```{r stocks-table}
stocks_tbl <- gics[
  gics$DA441_column_name %in% selected,
  c("company_ticker", "company_name", "GICS Sector Name")
]
stocks_tbl <- stocks_tbl[order(stocks_tbl[["GICS Sector Name"]], stocks_tbl$company_ticker), ]
colnames(stocks_tbl) <- c("Ticker", "Company", "GICS Sector")
idx <- rle(as.character(stocks_tbl$`GICS Sector`)) # sector run-lengths (table is sector-sorted)
kable(stocks_tbl[, c("Ticker", "Company")],
  booktabs = TRUE, longtable = TRUE,
  caption = "Our (Group 12's) 100 sampled companies, grouped by GICS sector."
) |>
  kable_styling(full_width = FALSE, font_size = 9, latex_options = c("repeat_header")) |>
  pack_rows(index = setNames(idx$lengths, idx$values))
```

```{=latex}
\clearpage
\newpage
```

# Coursework 2: Supplementary analyses {#apd-b}

## Robustness checks {#apx-b-robustness}

```{r apx-b-helpers, include=FALSE}
# Lightweight OOS check used by the sensitivity analyses: a single 5-fold CV
# of the plain Gamma GLM (glm2) under a given preprocessing configuration.
# Fixed seed so all configurations face identical fold partitions.
quick_cv_glm <- function(veh_cap = 9, bm_cap = 150, k_smooth = 20,
                         driv_df = 3, veh_df = 3, seed = 1) {
  # Formula must match the spline df of the configuration
  sp_terms <- c(paste0("DrivAge_Sp", seq_len(driv_df)),
                paste0("VehAge_Sp",  seq_len(veh_df)))
  fml <- reformulate(c("BonusMalus_Log", "VehPower_Log", "Density_Log",
                       sp_terms, "VehGas", "TE_Region", "TE_Area",
                       "TE_VehBrand"), response = "Severity")
  set.seed(seed)
  folds <- sample(rep(1:5, length.out = nrow(data_sev)))
  out <- sapply(1:5, function(f) {
    fe <- build_features(data_sev[folds != f, ], data_sev[folds == f, ],
                         veh_cap = veh_cap, bm_cap = bm_cap,
                         driv_df = driv_df, veh_df = veh_df,
                         k_smooth = k_smooth)
    Xtr <- model.matrix(fml, fe$train); Xte <- model.matrix(fml, fe$test)
    m <- glm.fit2(x = Xtr, y = fe$train$Severity, weights = fe$train$ClaimNb,
                  family = family_type, control = control_list)
    cf <- m$coefficients; cf[is.na(cf)] <- 0
    mu <- exp(as.numeric(Xte %*% cf))
    cap95 <- quantile(fe$train$Severity, 0.95)
    c(RMSE_W95 = calc_rmse_win(fe$test$Severity, mu, cap95),
      Gini = NormalisedWeightedGini(fe$test$Severity, fe$test$ClaimNb, mu),
      AE = sum(fe$test$Severity) / sum(mu))
  })
  rowMeans(out)
}

# Full-data Gamma GLM fit for AIC comparisons (glm2 wrapper returns a glm
# object, so AIC() is available; glm.fit2 alone does not report AIC).
fit_full_glm <- function(veh_cap = 9, bm_cap = 150, k_smooth = 20,
                         driv_df = 3, veh_df = 3, standardise = TRUE) {
  sp_terms <- c(paste0("DrivAge_Sp", seq_len(driv_df)),
                paste0("VehAge_Sp",  seq_len(veh_df)))
  fml <- reformulate(c("BonusMalus_Log", "VehPower_Log", "Density_Log",
                       sp_terms, "VehGas", "TE_Region", "TE_Area",
                       "TE_VehBrand"), response = "Severity")
  fe <- build_features(data_sev, NULL, veh_cap = veh_cap, bm_cap = bm_cap,
                       driv_df = driv_df, veh_df = veh_df,
                       k_smooth = k_smooth, standardise = standardise)
  glm2::glm2(fml, data = fe$train, family = family_type, weights = ClaimNb,
             control = glm.control(maxit = 250, epsilon = 1e-6))
}
```

### Sensitivity to the VehPower and BonusMalus caps {#apx-b-caps}

The preprocessing caps `VehPower` at 9 and `BonusMalus` at 150 before the log
transform. Both cut-offs are varied here with all other choices held constant.
The results are evaluated using the full-data Gamma GLM's Akaike information
criterion (AIC) alongside a single 5-fold CV of winsorised RMSE (95%),
normalised Gini and the AE ratio.

```{r apx-b-caps-run, include=FALSE}
veh_grid <- c(6, 9, 12, Inf)
bm_grid <- c(100, 150, 200, Inf)
caps_veh <- t(sapply(veh_grid, function(vc) {
  c(AIC = AIC(fit_full_glm(veh_cap = vc)), quick_cv_glm(veh_cap = vc))
}))
caps_bm <- t(sapply(bm_grid, function(bc) {
  c(AIC = AIC(fit_full_glm(bm_cap = bc)), quick_cv_glm(bm_cap = bc))
}))
caps_tab <- data.frame(
  Parameter = c(
    rep("VehPower cap", length(veh_grid)),
    rep("BonusMalus cap", length(bm_grid))
  ),
  Cap = c(
    ifelse(is.finite(veh_grid), veh_grid, "none"),
    ifelse(is.finite(bm_grid), bm_grid, "none")
  ),
  rbind(caps_veh, caps_bm), check.names = FALSE
)

# policies the 150 and 100 caps touch
n_bm_gt150 <- sum(data_sev$BonusMalus > 150)   
n_bm_gt100 <- sum(data_sev$BonusMalus > 100)  
```

```{r apx-b-caps-table}
kable(caps_tab[, -1],
  row.names = FALSE, booktabs = TRUE,
  digits = c(NA, 1, 0, 3, 3),
  col.names = c(
    "Cap", "AIC (full fit)", "RMSE W95 (CV)",
    "Gini norm (CV)", "AE (CV)"
  ),
  caption = "The effect of VehPower and BonusMalus cut-offs on the Gamma GLM (the values used were 9 and 150 respectively). AIC from the full-data fit; the remaining columns from a single 5-fold CV on a common fold partition."
) |>
  kable_styling(
    full_width = FALSE, position = "center",
    latex_options = "hold_position"
  ) |>
  pack_rows("VehPower cap (BonusMalus = 150)", 1, length(veh_grid)) |>
  pack_rows(
    "BonusMalus cap (VehPower = 9)", length(veh_grid) + 1,
    length(veh_grid) + length(bm_grid)
  )
```

As shown in Table \@ref(tab:apx-b-caps-table), across all eight configurations
the AIC spans barely a single unit
(`r fmt(min(caps_tab$AIC), 1)` to `r fmt(max(caps_tab$AIC), 1)`),
winsorised RMSE moves by 
`r round(max(caps_tab$RMSE_W95) - min(caps_tab$RMSE_W95))`
euros
(€`r round(min(caps_tab$RMSE_W95))` to €`r round(max(caps_tab$RMSE_W95))`), and the
AE ratio is effectively constant (`r fmt(min(caps_tab$AE), 3)` to
`r fmt(max(caps_tab$AE), 3)`). 
This shows that the defaults are neither
favoured nor penalised and the model is insensitive to where these two
variables are truncated. The `BonusMalus` cap matters less than the
`VehPower` cap, with caps of 150, 200 and "none" resulting in identical figures. This is because no policy (in our `r fmt(n_pol, 0)` policies) carries a `BonusMalus` score above 150, so raising or removing the ceiling does not change the model. The only visible movement comes from the tighter cap of 100, which touches the `r fmt(n_bm_gt100, 0)` policies scoring above it and nudges RMSE up to `r round(caps_tab$RMSE_W95[caps_tab$Cap == "100"])`
(versus `r round(caps_tab$RMSE_W95[caps_tab$Cap == "150"])` at the default). Scores above 100 are the malus-loaded, higher-risk policies, so cutting at 100 compresses real differentiation among the worst drivers. The
normalised Gini stays negative throughout, consistent with the finding in \@ref(b1-raw). Policy-level severity carries no rankable out-of-sample signal at this sample size so its small movements across the grid are not informative.

### Spline flexibility: three versus four degrees of freedom {#apx-b-splines}


```{r apx-b-splines-run, include=FALSE}
sp_grid <- expand.grid(driv_df = c(3, 4), veh_df = c(3, 4))
sp_tab <- cbind(sp_grid, t(apply(sp_grid, 1, function(g) {
  m <- fit_full_glm(driv_df = g["driv_df"], veh_df = g["veh_df"])
  c(
    AIC = AIC(m), Deviance = deviance(m), p = length(coef(m)),
    quick_cv_glm(driv_df = g["driv_df"], veh_df = g["veh_df"])
  )
})))
```

```{r apx-b-splines-table}
kable(sp_tab,
  row.names = FALSE, booktabs = TRUE,
  align = "c",
  digits = c(0, 0, 1, 1, 0, 0, 3, 3),
  col.names = c(
    "DrivAge", "VehAge", "AIC", "Deviance", "Params",
    "RMSE W95 (CV)", "Gini norm (CV)", "AE (CV)"
  ),
  caption = "Comparison of spline flexibilities for driver and vehicle age, evaluated via full-data AIC, deviance, and 5-fold cross-validation metrics."
) |>
  kable_styling(
    full_width = FALSE, position = "center",
    latex_options = "hold_position"
  ) |>
  add_header_above(c("Degrees of freedom" = 2, " " = 6))
```


Increasing the natural-spline degrees of freedom for `DrivAge` and `VehAge` from
3 to 4 adds one internal knot, and one parameter, per variable. As deviance
alone fails to account for overfitting, we opted to primarily evaluate these
models using AIC to penalise the additional parameters, paired with 5-fold
cross-validation to assess true out-of-sample performance (Table 
\@ref(tab:apx-b-splines-table)).

Adding a knot to `DrivAge` (while holding `VehAge` at 3) yields no benefit. The deviance remains completely unchanged at `r fmt(sp_tab$Deviance[sp_tab$driv_df==4 & sp_tab$veh_df==3], 1)`. As a result the AIC increases strictly due to the parameter penalty (from `r fmt(sp_tab$AIC[sp_tab$driv_df==3 & sp_tab$veh_df==3], 1)` to `r fmt(sp_tab$AIC[sp_tab$driv_df==4 & sp_tab$veh_df==3], 1)`), and the winsorised RMSE worsens from `r round(sp_tab$RMSE_W95[sp_tab$driv_df==3 & sp_tab$veh_df==3])` to `r round(sp_tab$RMSE_W95[sp_tab$driv_df==4 & sp_tab$veh_df==3])`. This indicates the extra driver-age flexibility is simply fitting noise. 

Adding a knot to `VehAge` in isolation does improve the model. Deviance falls to `r fmt(sp_tab$Deviance[sp_tab$driv_df==3 & sp_tab$veh_df==4], 1)`, AIC decreases by roughly nine units (to `r fmt(sp_tab$AIC[sp_tab$driv_df==3 & sp_tab$veh_df==4], 1)`), and winsorised RMSE improves to `r round(sp_tab$RMSE_W95[sp_tab$driv_df==3 & sp_tab$veh_df==4])`. 
However this performance gain is modest (approximately a `r fmt(100 * (1 - sp_tab$RMSE_W95[sp_tab$driv_df==3 & sp_tab$veh_df==4] / sp_tab$RMSE_W95[sp_tab$driv_df==3 & sp_tab$veh_df==3]), 1)`% improvement in winsorised RMSE) and serves better as an acknowledged limitation of the current specification rather than a mandate to re-run the full 500-run cross-validation study. The normalised Gini coefficient also remains negative across all configurations, showing that the increased flexibility provides no additional out-of-sample ranking power. We therefore retain three degrees of freedom for both variables, matching the specification of the released practice code and @Asimit2025GLMsolsviaShrinkage, and note the additional `VehAge` knot as a candidate refinement for a larger study.

<!-- Despite this, we retain three degrees of freedom for both variables in the main analysis. This ensures symmetry between the age effects and aligns with the baseline specification established by @Asimit2025GLMsolsviaShrinkage.  -->

### Sensitivity to the target-encoding smoother $\kappa$ {#apx-b-ksmooth}

```{r apx-b-ksmooth-run, include=FALSE}
k_grid <- c(5, 10, 20, 50, 100)
ks_tab <- data.frame(
  kappa = k_grid,
  t(sapply(k_grid, function(k) quick_cv_glm(k_smooth = k))),
  check.names = FALSE
)
```

The credibility weight in the target encoding is defined as 
$$
\lambda = \frac{n_{\text{group}}}{n_{\text{group}} + \kappa}
$$
with a default parameter of
$\kappa = 20$. A smaller $\kappa$ places more trust in sparse group means
(risking overfitting), whereas a larger $\kappa$ shrinks every group toward the
global mean (risking underfitting). Because in-sample fit inherently rewards a
smaller $\kappa$, we evaluate the comparison entirely out-of-sample.

As shown in Table \@ref(tab:apx-b-ksmooth-table), out-of-sample performance
remains essentially flat across all values of $\kappa$ with no single value performing materially better than the default. Winsorised RMSE varies
by only a couple of euros across the entire grid (from
`r round(min(ks_tab$RMSE_W95))` at $\kappa = 5$ to 
`r round(max(ks_tab$RMSE_W95))` at $\kappa = 100$), and the AE ratio is
virtually unchanged (ranging from `r fmt(min(ks_tab$AE), 3)` to 
`r fmt(max(ks_tab$AE), 3)`).  Interestingly, a small $\kappa$ does not lead to
overfitting in this instance. The lowest winsorised RMSE occurs at the minimum
tested value of $\kappa = 5$ suggesting that trusting sparse brand or region
cells does not penalise the model at the current sample size. The only monotonic
trend appears in the normalised Gini coefficient, which becomes steadily less
negative as $\kappa$ increases (from `r fmt(ks_tab$Gini[ks_tab$kappa==5], 3)` to
`r fmt(ks_tab$Gini[ks_tab$kappa==100], 3)`). However, since every recorded value
remains negative, this trend merely reflects the encoding being pulled more
aggressively toward the global mean, rather than representing a genuine
improvement in ranking power. 

```{r apx-b-ksmooth-table}
kable(ks_tab,
  row.names = FALSE, booktabs = TRUE, digits = c(0, 0, 3, 3),
  col.names = c("$\\kappa$", "RMSE W95 (CV)", "Gini norm (CV)", "AE (CV)"),
  escape = FALSE,
  caption = "Evaluating the Gamma GLM's sensitivity to the target-encoding smoothing parameter $\\kappa$. Performance is measured via a single 5-fold cross-validation on a shared partition, with 20 serving as the default value."
) |>
  kable_styling(
    full_width = FALSE, position = "center",
    latex_options = "hold_position"
  )
```

### Standardisation {#apx-b-scaling}

For `glm2`, `savvyGLM` and `glmnet` the fitted values depend on the data only
through the linear predictor $x_i^\top\beta$. Scaling a column by $s$ rescales
its coefficient by $1/s$, leaving $\hat\mu_i$ unchanged. We note also that
`glmnet` applies standardisation internally by default, ensuring that
penalisation is applied on a common scale regardless of the input format.

```{r apx-b-scaling-check, echo=FALSE}
m_scaled <- fit_full_glm(standardise = TRUE)
m_raw    <- fit_full_glm(standardise = FALSE)
# max(abs(fitted(m_scaled) - fitted(m_raw))) 
```

To investigate this empirically, the full-data Gamma GLM was fitted with and
without standardisation. The maximum absolute difference across all 
`r fmt(n_pol, 0)` fitted severities is approximately 
`r formatC(max(abs(fitted(m_scaled) - fitted(m_raw))), format = "e", digits = 1)`
(a negligible discrepancy attributable to floating-point rounding error). This
confirms that standardisation in this context serves as a presentational
convenience, retained to mirror the released practice code, rather than a
substantive modelling choice that affects the results.

## Convergence and computational notes {#apx-b-convergence}

As an observational note, we encountered two types of warnings during the 
`r fmt(n_runs, 0)`-run loop. Both originate from designed safeguards within the
fitting algorithms rather than from data anomalies or implementation errors.
Neither invalidates the reported results, but they are of technical interest and
are described briefly below for completeness, alongside our interpretation.

glmnet: algorithm did not converge
:   This appears when `glmnet` is asked to fit a Gamma model with a log link.
    For each candidate penalty strength, `glmnet` runs a small iterative fitting
    routine that is allowed up to 25 passes by default. With heavy-tailed
    severity data, the fits at the very lightly penalised end of the range
    occasionally need more than 25 passes, so they stop early and report it.
    This does not affect the final model. The penalty is chosen by
    cross-validation, and a fit that has not settled scores poorly and is passed
    over. We checked that the penalty finally selected never comes from that
    unsettled end of the range. 

savvyGLM / glm.fit2: step size truncated / cannot correct step size
:   The shrinkage GLMs use a fitting routine (glm.fit2)
    built around a simple safety check. At each step, if the proposed update
    would make the fit worse rather than better, the update is halved and tried
    again until it improves, and each time this happens a message is logged. 
    This is common with a Gamma/log model when the predictors are correlated.
    The "cannot correct step size" message means the routine used up its allowed
    halvings at that step and carried on from the best result it had so far.
    What actually matters is whether the fit settles in the end, and we record
    that for every fit. Across the `r fmt(n_runs, 0)` runs, the outcome is
    summarised in the table below. The number of passes stays far below the
    ceiling of 250, ranging from 
    `r fmt(mean(final_results$NoIt[final_results$Model == "SR"], na.rm = TRUE), 1)`
    for SR to 
    `r fmt(mean(final_results$NoIt[final_results$Model == "GLM2"], na.rm = TRUE), 1)` for GLM2, and every fit in this family (GLM2 and the six
    savvyGLM estimators) settles in all `r fmt(n_runs, 0)` runs. The glmnet
    models (RR and EN) do not expose a comparable per-fit flag, shown as a dash
    in the table, and their iteration counts measure coordinate-descent passes,
    so neither column is compared with the IRLS family.


```{r apx-b-conv-table}
noit_tab <- aggregate(NoIt ~ Model, final_results, mean, na.rm = TRUE)
conv_pct <- aggregate(final_results$Converged,
  by = list(Model = final_results$Model),
  FUN = function(x) {
    if (all(is.na(x))) {
      NA_real_
    } else {
      100 * mean(x, na.rm = TRUE)
    }
  }
)
names(conv_pct)[2] <- "Converged"
conv_kbl <- merge(conv_pct, noit_tab, by = "Model", all = TRUE)
conv_kbl <- conv_kbl[match(model_names, conv_kbl$Model), ]
kable(conv_kbl,
  row.names = FALSE, booktabs = TRUE, digits = c(NA, 1, 1),
  col.names = c("Model", "Converged (\\% of runs)", "Mean NoIt"),
  escape = FALSE,
  caption = paste0(
    "Convergence across the ", fmt(n_runs, 0), " evaluation runs. glmnet does not expose a per-fit convergence flag, shown as a dash. Its NoIt column counts coordinate-descent passes rather than IRLS iterations, so the two are not directly comparable."
  )
) |>
  kable_styling(
    full_width = FALSE, position = "center",
    latex_options = "hold_position"
  )
```


```{=latex}
\clearpage
\newpage
```

# Interactive R Shiny Dashboard {#apd-dashboard}

To enable an interactive exploration of (stuff), an R Shiny dashboard was constructed
and deployed to shinyapps.io. The application is available via:

- TBC

```{r appendix-dashboard-1, eval=FALSE, echo=FALSE, purl=FALSE, out.width="90%", fig.cap="TBI."}
knitr::include_graphics("fig/appendix-dashboard-1.png")
```

<!-- BE note: taken out as we are submitting R file separately -->
<!-- Use: `knitr::purl("cs1-group07.rmd", documentation = 0)` to generate r file -->
<!-- Use: `tools::showNonASCIIfile("cs1-group07.rmd")` to debug -->
<!-- ## R Code Documentation -->
<!-- ```{r ref.label=knitr::all_labels(), echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}``` -->

## Reproducibility & accessibility

An accessible HTML version of this report is available via a public GitHub page:

- https://github.com/ytterbiu/smm069-assignment_g12

This report was created in R Markdown. The source code is open source and is
available via:

- https://ytterbiu.github.io/smm069-assignment_g12/

Changes made to this document were tracked using Git and are also available via
the same repository (https://github.com/ytterbiu/smm069-assignment_g12/commits/main/). Please note that this commit history reflects
updates made to the project repository and is not a comprehensive timeline of
all contributions by group members.

# Generative AI declaration {#apd:genai}

Generative AI (GenAI) tools were used throughout this project to assist with
project planning, methodological validation, debugging, and the development of
the supplementary interactive dashboard described in Appendix
\@ref(apd-dashboard). In practice, these tools functioned as an additional
technical resource, providing immediate feedback, troubleshooting code, and
discussing conceptual assumptions.

## Tools used {-}

Google Gemini 3 Pro (Deep Research), Claude (Anthropic) Opus 4.8 & Fable 5.

## Prompts used {-}

The GenAI tools were provided with the coursework task sheet and
extracts from our R code.
A non-exhaustive list of prompts used is shown below. We opted to include at
least one example of each *type* of prompt used, rather than a list of all
prompts. For example, if we entered the prompts "How can we put two ggplots side
by side?" and "How to remove grey background on a ggplot", we would include only
the first of these in the list below, as both relate to plotting issues (and are
similar to querying with a traditional search engine).

- "...the top of both graphs in R are slightly different. (Pasting R code.) Can
  this be fixed for the plot area?"
- "Explain the repeated Sharpe-ratio values..."
- "Help to identify the key points here"
- "How can this error be tracked down in an Rmd file? `! Text line contains an
  invalid character. l.1 ^^@^^@...`"
- "Are there any issues with this app code? Suggest corrections for any potential bugs or errors within the server side code" 

## Use of GenAI outputs & subsequent revisions {-}

The GenAI tools served as a consultative resource for the group. Notably, the AI
output:

- Helped debug plotting issues and R Markdown compilation errors.
- Commented on the structure of R code snippets and the report structure.
- Assisted with converting draft content between document formats.
- Reviewed elements of the R Shiny app code, identifying bugs and
  design issues.

## Changes made {-}

All AI suggestions were critically reviewed and debated by the group before
implementation. For example:

- While the AI suggested code for complex layouts, the specific aesthetic
  choices and annotations were manually adjusted to ensure clarity.
- Where the AI proposed specific interpretations, these were cross-referenced
  with academic literature (notably @Asimit15062026 and the lecture notes).
- In the case of the Shiny dashboard, the AI corrections were evaluated
  against the original code by the group.  Each identified issue was
  assessed individually.
- The final R code for implementation was written and debugged by the group,
  with AI snippets being treated as drafts rather than final solutions.

While GenAI was treated as a collaborator, the final analysis, statistical
interpretation, and the written content of this report were generated and
verified by all group members.

<!-- [^utility-theory]: -->
<!--     Described in more detail in the appendix (\S\@ref(apd-utility-theory)). -->

<!-- [^utility-skew]: -->
<!--     This is demonstrated in the appendix (\S\@ref(apd-utility-theory) and Table -->
<!--     \@ref(tab:exact-utility-table)) where we show that a constant relative risk -->
<!--     aversion (CRRA) utility function with $\gamma > 0$ mathematically confirms -->
<!--     that for risk-averse investors asset 1 results in a lower expected utility -->
<!--     that asset 2. -->
